cards.front: What is Coronary Artery Disease (CAD)?
cards.back: Coronary Artery Disease, also known as ischemic heart disease, is a condition where plaque builds up in the coronary arteries, restricting blood flow to the heart. It is the leading cause of death globally, accounting for 16% of total deaths.

cards.front: What are the global mortality statistics for cardiovascular disease?
cards.back: According to WHO 2019 statistics, cardiovascular disease is the commonest cause of mortality accounting for 18.5 million deaths annually, far exceeding cancer (10 million) and respiratory diseases (under 4 million).

cards.front: What is the double burden of disease in South Africa and low/middle-income countries?
cards.back: The double burden refers to the simultaneous high prevalence of both infectious diseases (tuberculosis, AIDS) and non-communicable diseases (lifestyle-related conditions like CAD, hypertension, diabetes).

cards.front: What is the most lethal manifestation of Coronary Artery Disease?
cards.back: Myocardial infarction (heart attack) is the most lethal and dangerous manifestation of CAD. It is a medical emergency caused by complete or near-complete blockage of a coronary artery.

cards.front: What is the key message about CAD prevention and modification?
cards.back: CAD is a disease of lifestyle that can be modified through choices about diet and physical activity. It can also be managed with pharmacological therapies and mechanical interventions (angioplasty, stenting, bypass surgery). Leading a judicious lifestyle can delay or prevent CAD onset.

cards.front: What are the main clinical manifestations of CAD?
cards.back: The main manifestations are: (1) Stable/Chronic coronary syndrome (stable angina), (2) Acute coronary syndrome including ST-elevation MI and non-ST elevation MI, (3) Unstable angina, (4) Silent ischemia, and (5) Sudden cardiac death.

cards.front: Why is CAD now called Chronic Coronary Syndrome?
cards.back: CAD is called chronic coronary syndrome because the disease starts early (in teen years or even earlier) but remains clinically silent for many years. Evidence of disease appears as fatty streaks in arteries from childhood, becoming manifest typically after age 35, most commonly between ages 50-80.

cards.front: What is the difference between ST-elevation MI (STEMI) and non-ST elevation MI?
cards.back: In STEMI, the thrombus causes total occlusion of the coronary vessel, making it a medical emergency with rapid myocardial death. In non-STEMI, the thrombus is not completely occlusive, allowing some blood flow; it is less time-dependent but still requires urgent treatment.

cards.front: What are non-modifiable risk factors for CAD?
cards.back: Non-modifiable risk factors are: (1) Age - prevalence increases after age 35; (2) Male gender - higher lifetime risk; (3) Family history of early CAD (father/brother <55 years or mother/sister <65 years); (4) Certain ethnicities including Indian, Afrikaans, Ashkenazi Jewish, and Scottish populations.

cards.front: What are the lifetime risks for CAD development by gender after age 40?
cards.back: Men have a 49% lifetime risk of developing CAD after age 40 years, while women have a 32% lifetime risk. After menopause, the prevalence becomes equivalent between genders.

cards.front: What is the relationship between family history and CAD risk?
cards.back: Family history of CAD below age 50 years indicates increased risk. Specifically: if father or brother diagnosed before 55 years, or mother or sister diagnosed before 65 years of age, these individuals are at additional risk.

cards.front: What is the most prevalent modifiable risk factor for CAD?
cards.back: Hypertension is the most prevalent modifiable risk factor, with approximately one out of every three individuals globally having hypertension.

cards.front: How does diabetes affect CAD risk and outcomes?
cards.back: Diabetes mellitus (both Type 1 and Type 2) increases the rate of CAD by more than 2.5 times compared to non-diabetics. Although hypertension and hyperlipidemia are more common, diabetes is a more severe condition, leading to greater disease burden and poorer outcomes in CAD patients.

cards.front: What is the CAD risk increase from secondhand smoke exposure?
cards.back: Non-smokers exposed to secondhand smoking have a 25-30% increased risk of CAD compared to those never exposed to cigarette smoke. This is why smoking is banned in public areas - not to protect smokers, but to protect non-smokers.

cards.front: What dietary factors increase CAD risk?
cards.back: Dietary risk factors include: (1) Added sugars in soft drinks and sweetened beverages - associated with >20% higher MI risk; (2) Saturated and trans fats; (3) Excess red meat and processed meats (sausages, cured meats); (4) Overall poor diet quality.

cards.front: What is the relationship between obesity and CAD?
cards.back: Obesity is an independent risk factor for CAD and also increases the risk of developing other risk factors like hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and Type 2 diabetes. This effect is particularly pronounced with abdominal obesity.

cards.front: What are less recognized risk factors for CAD?
cards.back: Less recognized risk factors include: (1) Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) - now the commonest cause of cirrhosis; (2) Chronic kidney disease - increases CAD incidence and mortality; (3) Inflammatory conditions (lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease); (4) HIV infection.

cards.front: What is the Framingham Risk Score and what are its components?
cards.back: The Framingham Risk Score is one of the oldest risk assessment tools. It considers: age, total cholesterol, HDL cholesterol, systolic blood pressure (treated or untreated), and smoking status. It provides a 10-year likelihood of developing cardiovascular disease including CAD, stroke, and peripheral artery disease.

cards.front: Why is the Framingham Risk Score not useful in certain populations?
cards.back: The Framingham Risk Score is not useful in diabetics or those with familial hyperlipidemia because these individuals are already at higher risk simply by virtue of having these conditions. Risk scoring is unnecessary when baseline risk is already elevated.

cards.front: What are the Framingham Risk Score categories and their implications?
cards.back: Risk score categories: (1) Score <3 = Low risk; (2) Score 3-15 = Moderate risk; (3) Score >15, especially >30 = High risk requiring pharmacotherapy in addition to lifestyle measures. Scores >30 require aggressive risk factor control to minimize MI and death risk.

cards.front: Who are considered very high-risk for cardiovascular events without needing risk scoring?
cards.back: Individuals at very high risk who do NOT require risk scoring include: (1) Those with established atherosclerotic disease in any vascular territory; (2) Type 2 diabetes with other risk factors; (3) Type 1 diabetes with proteinuria; (4) Familial/genetic hyperlipidemia; (5) Severe chronic kidney disease (stages 4-5); (6) Asymptomatic individuals with evidence of atherosclerotic plaques on imaging.

cards.front: What is the significance of atherosclerotic disease in multiple vascular territories?
cards.back: If atherosclerotic disease is established in one vascular territory (coronary, cerebrovascular, peripheral arterial), the likelihood of having it in other vascular beds is high. This is why individuals with established atherosclerosis in any location automatically have very high overall cardiovascular risk.

cards.front: What defines familial/genetic hyperlipidemia risk?
cards.back: Severe familial hyperlipidemia automatically places individuals at high CAD risk. It is defined by: total cholesterol >7.5 mmol/L or LDL cholesterol >5 mmol/L (LDL is 'bad cholesterol'). This population is particularly common among people of Dutch descent in South Africa, with approximately 300,000 severely affected individuals.

cards.front: What is the pathophysiology of chronic coronary syndrome?
cards.back: In chronic coronary syndrome, a plaque composed of lipid, inflammatory cells, and smooth muscle cells causes >50-70% lumen stenosis in a coronary artery. This narrowing limits blood flow downstream, manifesting as ischemic chest pain (angina) only when the vessel is narrowed >70%.

cards.front: What is the pathophysiology of acute coronary syndrome in STEMI?
cards.back: In STEMI, a plaque ruptures and a large thrombus forms on the rupture site, causing complete occlusion of the coronary vessel. This is a true emergency because there is no blood flow downstream and myocytes rapidly begin to die (myocardial infarction).

cards.front: What is the pathophysiology of acute coronary syndrome in non-STEMI?
cards.back: In non-STEMI, a long-standing plaque fissures or ruptures and a thrombus forms, but the thrombus is not completely occlusive. Stenosis increases from 50-70% to ~90%, but some blood flow remains. Infarction occurs when bits of the clot break off and block smaller downstream vessels.

cards.front: What is stable angina and how is it defined?
cards.back: Stable angina is now called Chronic Coronary Syndrome. It is defined as angina where the symptom characteristics have remained unchanged for the preceding 60 days. This distinction is important to differentiate it from unstable angina, where symptoms are changing or new.

cards.front: How is angina diagnosed?
cards.back: Angina is diagnosed by history and history alone. The diagnosis is made based on the patient's description of symptoms, not on any investigation or test. ECG, stress testing, or imaging can support the diagnosis but are not required for diagnosis.

cards.front: What are the three criteria for typical angina?
cards.back: Typical angina must satisfy all three criteria: (1) Chest discomfort described as constriction in the front of chest, radiating to neck, jaw, shoulder, or arm(s); (2) Provoked by physical exertion or emotional stress; (3) Rapidly relieved by rest or nitrates within 5 minutes.

cards.front: What is atypical angina?
cards.back: Atypical angina (probable angina) occurs when a patient meets only two of the three typical angina criteria.

cards.front: What is non-anginal chest pain?
cards.back: Non-anginal chest pain is when only one of the three typical angina criteria is satisfied.

cards.front: What is the Canadian Cardiovascular Society Classification of Angina?
cards.back: The CCS Classification grades angina severity: (1) Class I - Angina only with very strenuous exertion; (2) Class II - Angina with moderate exertion; (3) Class III - Angina with mild exertion; (4) Class IV - Angina at rest. This is used to convey angina severity universally.

cards.front: What is the significance of the age 35 in CAD?
cards.back: Age 35 is significant because CAD prevalence increases after this age. While the disease begins in early teen years with fatty streaks, clinical manifestations typically appear after age 35, with peak incidence between ages 50-80.

cards.front: What evidence shows that CAD begins in childhood?
cards.back: Post-mortem examinations of young individuals (aged 19-20) who died during wartime already showed early markings of atherosclerotic disease in the form of fatty streaks in coronary arteries, proving the disease starts very early in life.

cards.front: Why is treatment of CAD lifelong?
cards.back: Treatment is lifelong because CAD is a chronic disease process that continues throughout life. Even if a plaque rupture is managed and the vessel is reopened (by treatment or healing), the vessel remains abnormal. The underlying atherosclerotic disease persists and requires ongoing management.

cards.front: What percentage of patients with CAD present with angina?
cards.back: Approximately 50% of patients with CAD present with angina (stable or unstable), making it the most common initial manifestation of the disease.

cards.front: At what percentage stenosis does a coronary artery begin to restrict blood flow?
cards.back: A coronary artery must be narrowed (stenosed) by more than 50-70% to meaningfully restrict blood flow downstream and cause clinical manifestations like angina.

cards.front: What percentage stenosis is clinically symptomatic in typical CAD?
cards.back: Clinical manifestation of ischemic heart disease typically occurs once a coronary vessel is 70% or more narrowed. At this level of narrowing, blood flow is sufficiently restricted to cause ischemia and symptoms.

cards.front: What is the role of inflammation in CAD pathophysiology?
cards.back: CAD is fundamentally an inflammatory process. The atherosclerotic plaque contains inflammatory cells along with lipids and smooth muscle cells. This inflammatory nature explains why inflammatory conditions (lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, IBD) increase CAD risk.

cards.front: What diagnostic modalities are used for CAD diagnosis?
cards.back: Diagnostic modalities include: (1) ECG - may show previous MI or changes with stress; (2) Stress testing - treadmill, pharmacological (dobutamine), or nuclear stress tests; (3) CT coronary angiography - visualizes plaque burden, quantifies stenosis percentage, and demonstrates coronary calcification.

cards.front: What is the relationship between radiation pattern and angina severity?
cards.back: The radiation pattern of chest pain (to neck, jaw, shoulder, or arm) is part of typical angina description. This characteristic radiation helps distinguish angina from other causes of chest pain and is one of the three diagnostic criteria.

cards.front: What does ST segment depression of more than 1mm indicate, especially if horizontal?
cards.back: Highly suggestive of underlying coronary obstruction.

cards.front: What is the significance of ST elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI)?
cards.back: A medical emergency where myocytes are under threat and begin to die after 5 minutes without blood supply and oxygen delivery.

cards.front: What are the diagnostic criteria for ST elevation in limb leads vs chest leads for STEMI?
cards.back: Limb leads (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF): require at least 1mm elevation. Chest leads (precordial): require at least 2mm elevation.

cards.front: What does 'tombstone' pattern represent on ECG?
cards.back: A pattern of convex ST elevation (often 5-15mm) seen in anterior STEMI, named for its appearance.

cards.front: What is reciprocal ST depression in the context of myocardial infarction?
cards.back: ST depression in leads opposite to those showing ST elevation; occurs when infarction affects one region and helps confirm the diagnosis.

cards.front: When is exercise stress testing contraindicated?
cards.back: In very high-risk patients (with typical anginal symptoms and multiple risk factors) and very low-risk patients (young individuals with no risk factors), as it may provoke ischemic events or is unhelpful.

cards.front: What is the Brusport protocol for exercise stress testing?
cards.back: Standard protocol where the treadmill increases speed and incline every 3 minutes; has 7 stages, with most individuals expected to complete at least stages 1 and 2 (6 minutes of exercise).

cards.front: What ECG changes during exercise stress testing warrant immediate cessation?
cards.back: ST elevation (ominous), ST depression greater than 1.5-2mm, or inability to increase heart rate adequately.

cards.front: What are the two main classes of antiplatelet drugs used in coronary artery disease?
cards.back: Aspirin and ADP receptor antagonists (P2Y12 inhibitors) such as clopidogrel.

cards.front: Name the main classes of lipid-lowering agents used in CAD management, in order of escalation.
cards.back: Statins (first line), azetamide (if statin targets not reached), and PCSK9 inhibitors (if further reduction needed).

cards.front: When are beta-blockers particularly beneficial in coronary artery disease?
cards.back: In patients with prior myocardial infarction and LV dysfunction (ejection fraction <40%), and in those with high heart rates or angina, as they reduce myocardial oxygen demand.

cards.front: What are contemporary disease-modifying therapies in coronary artery disease beyond statins?
cards.back: PCSK9 inhibitors, bempedoic acid, inclisiran (SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 agonists for diabetic patients), inflammatory modulators (e.g., Ziltivekumab, Canakinumab), and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone inhibitors.

cards.front: What is the target resting heart rate for patients on beta-blockers with stable angina?
cards.back: Below 60 bpm, ideally in the 50s.

cards.front: What non-dihydropyridine calcium antagonists are used when beta-blockers are contraindicated?
cards.back: Diltiazem and verapamil, as they also reduce heart rate, blood pressure, and force of LV contractility.

cards.front: What hemoglobin level should be maintained to ensure adequate myocardial oxygen delivery?
cards.back: Hemoglobin should be kept above 8 g/dL; anemia compromises oxygen delivery to cardiac myocytes.

cards.front: What oxygen saturation target is recommended for patients with coronary artery disease?
cards.back: Above 95%; supplemental oxygen should be given if saturation is lower.

cards.front: What are the key lifestyle modifications recommended for stable angina?
cards.back: Stop smoking, blood pressure <130/80 (ideally), LDL <1 mmol/L, HbA1c <7%, BMI <25, endurance training 30 min 5x/week, and resistance training for muscle maintenance.

cards.front: When should coronary angiography be performed in stable angina despite medical treatment?
cards.back: When patients remain severely symptomatic (class III-IV angina) despite medical therapy, or when stress testing shows high-risk features (early ST changes before stage 2, drop in BP, marked ST depression), or if there is LV dysfunction.

cards.front: What is the definition of LV dysfunction in the context of angina management?
cards.back: Ejection fraction below 40% is clearly LV dysfunction; EF >50-60% is considered normal.

cards.front: What are the two main revascularization strategies for CAD?
cards.back: Percutaneous coronary intervention (angioplasty with or without stenting) or coronary artery bypass surgery in those unsuitable for PCI.

cards.front: What is the difference between STEMI and non-STEMI acute coronary syndrome?
cards.back: STEMI involves total vessel occlusion by thrombus (medical emergency), while non-STEMI has partial patency with blood still able to flow (urgent but not emergent).

cards.front: What is the critical 'total ischemic time' target for STEMI management?
cards.back: Less than 3 hours from symptom onset to vessel opening; this is when maximum myocyte salvage occurs and lives are saved.

cards.front: How is the onset time for STEMI determined when pain is intermittent?
cards.back: STEMI timing begins from the onset of the pain that is most severe and ongoing, persisting beyond 30 minutes; stuttering pain is timed from the continuous phase.

cards.front: What are the two essential diagnostic criteria for STEMI in the acute setting?
cards.back: Typical ischemic chest pain ongoing for more than 30 minutes AND ST elevation on ECG (≥1mm in limb leads or ≥2mm in chest leads).

cards.front: When should troponin be awaited before diagnosing STEMI?
cards.back: Never; do not wait for troponin results in STEMI as it may take hours or days. Diagnosis is based solely on symptoms and ECG changes to enable rapid reperfusion.

cards.front: What should be done if ECG shows ST elevation is not clear when a patient presents early with chest pain?
cards.back: Repeat the ECG every 10 minutes until clear ST elevation is demonstrated, then act immediately; this maximizes diagnostic accuracy while maintaining urgency.

cards.front: What is the gold standard for reperfusion in STEMI?
cards.back: Primary percutaneous coronary intervention (angioplasty with stenting), which achieves 95% success rate in skilled hands.

cards.front: What is fibrinolysis and what is its time window in STEMI?
cards.back: Fibrinolysis is thrombolytic therapy given when PCI is not timeously available; it can be given up to 12 hours from symptom onset, ideally within 3 hours.

cards.front: Name the three types of fibrinolytic agents used in STEMI.
cards.back: Streptokinase (cheapest, infused over 1 hour), alteplase (more potent than streptokinase), and tenectoplase (best fibrinolytic agent with superior properties).

cards.front: What is the preferred fibrinolytic agent for STEMI, and why?
cards.back: Tenectoplase is preferred (most effective), followed by alteplase, then streptokinase. However, any fibrinolytic agent given timely is better than nothing. Tenectoplase is most expensive and not always available.

cards.front: What dual anti-platelet therapy is used in STEMI?
cards.back: Aspirin (chewed tablet for rapid absorption, then 100mg indefinitely) and clopidogrel loading dose 300mg followed by 75mg daily. Dual therapy continues for 1 year; then clopidogrel is stopped but aspirin continues for life.

cards.front: What anti-thrombin agent is preferred in STEMI and why?
cards.back: Enoxaparin is preferred over heparin because it is given subcutaneously (easier administration). Heparin requires continuous IV infusion and needs monitoring, making it more cumbersome.

cards.front: What is the minimum number of antithrombotic agents given in STEMI?
cards.back: A minimum of 4 antithrombotic agents: fibrinolytic agent + 2 antiplatelet agents (aspirin + clopidogrel) + 1 anticoagulant (enoxaparin or heparin).

cards.front: What is the success rate of primary angioplasty vs fibrinolytic therapy in STEMI?
cards.back: Primary angioplasty leads to 95% vessel opening in skilled hands. Fibrinolytic therapy achieves approximately 64% vessel patency.

cards.front: What is the timeline for endothelial incorporation of a metal stent?
cards.back: A metal stent is typically incorporated into the vessel wall by the endothelium over 1-3 months.

cards.front: What are the main adjunctive therapies in acute STEMI management?
cards.back: Pain relief (morphine or nitrates), high-dose statins (80mg atorvastatin vs 20mg chronically), ACE inhibitors (if EF <40%), beta blockers (if no heart block/bradycardia), oxygen (if saturation <90%), and treatment of comorbidities.

cards.front: What is a contraindication to fibrinolytic therapy in STEMI?
cards.back: Absolute contraindications include: hemorrhagic or recent stroke (<6 months), brain tumor, recent severe head trauma, major trauma/surgery within 2 weeks, active internal bleeding (peptic ulcer), aortic dissection, pregnancy, or prolonged CPR.

cards.front: What is the impact of uncontrolled hypertension on fibrinolytic eligibility?
cards.back: Severe uncontrolled hypertension is technically a contraindication, but it can be managed by starting IV nitrate infusion to lower BP below 150 systolic, after which fibrinolytic therapy can be safely given.

cards.front: What are the early complications of STEMI related to arrhythmias?
cards.back: Ventricular tachycardia (VT) or ventricular fibrillation (VF) can occur immediately when the vessel occludes and myocytes begin to die. If not treated immediately, this leads to sudden cardiac death.

cards.front: What heart block complications can occur with STEMI?
cards.back: Right coronary or multiple vessel occlusion can cause high-degree heart block (Type 2) or complete heart block, requiring emergency temporary pacemaker insertion.

cards.front: What percentage of left ventricular involvement leads to cardiogenic shock in STEMI?
cards.back: If >40% of the left ventricle is infarcted, left ventricular failure, pulmonary edema, or cardiogenic shock can occur.

cards.front: What mechanical complications can occur after STEMI?
cards.back: Acute mitral regurgitation (due to papillary muscle infarction), ventricular septal defect (from full-thickness septal rupture), and cardiac rupture (full-thickness wall rupture).

cards.front: What is the 30-day mortality in untreated STEMI?
cards.back: Untreated STEMI has 30-day mortality of approximately 40%. Fibrinolytic therapy reduces this to ~9%. Early intervention within 1 hour of occlusion reduces mortality to ~1%.

cards.front: What is the key difference between non-STEMI and unstable angina regarding myocardial necrosis?
cards.back: In non-STEMI, ischemia is severe enough to cause myocardial necrosis (elevated troponin or creatinine kinase). In unstable angina, there is no biochemical evidence of myocyte necrosis (troponins and CK are negative).

cards.front: What biomarker has most improved the management of coronary artery disease?
cards.back: Troponin is the go-to biomarker that has really altered CAD management. It has replaced creatinine kinase and is more sensitive for detecting myocardial necrosis.

cards.front: How is unstable angina defined in terms of symptom pattern change?
cards.back: Unstable angina means the pattern of angina has changed in the last 60 days: angina becoming more frequent, occurring with less effort (e.g., walking 50m on flat instead of climbing 2 flights of stairs), OR new-onset angina developing for the first time.

cards.front: What is the pathophysiology of unstable angina and non-STEMI?
cards.back: Yellow plaque with non-occlusive thrombus; bits of thrombus break off and occlude downstream vessels. The goal is to prevent the non-occlusive thrombus from becoming occlusive and causing infarction.

cards.front: What are the three risk categories for non-STEMI?
cards.back: Non-STEMI patients are risk-stratified into high, intermediate, and low risk based on symptoms, physical signs, ECG, and biomarkers. Higher risk correlates with poorer prognosis.

cards.front: What defines high-risk non-STEMI?
cards.back: High-risk non-STEMI includes: angina at rest in preceding 48 hours, hemodynamic instability (tachycardia >100, bradycardia <40, systolic BP <90), evidence of LV dysfunction (third heart sound, crackles, EF <40%), advanced elderly (>75 years), previous MI/angioplasty/bypass, other vascular disease (stroke, peripheral vascular disease), or positive troponins.

cards.front: What ECG findings are typically seen in non-STEMI and unstable angina?
cards.back: ECG may be completely normal in both conditions (>30% of patients). When abnormal: ST segment depression, transient ST elevation, bundle branch block, or T-wave inversion may be seen.

cards.front: What is the significance of ST segment depression in non-STEMI?
cards.back: ST segment depression strengthens diagnostic confidence for non-STEMI. It indicates ischemia and suggests higher risk, especially when more pronounced.

cards.front: How is low-risk unstable angina managed?
cards.back: Low-risk unstable angina (nausea pain, normal exam, normal ECG, normal troponins) is not admitted. Patient is treated with aspirin, statin, blood pressure/diabetes management. Risk is reassessed with symptoms and stress testing; additional imaging (angiogram) is decided based on results.

cards.front: What anti-ischemic therapy is used in high-risk non-STEMI?
cards.back: Nitrates (5-200 mcg depending on pain severity, if systolic BP >100, increased every 3-5 min until symptom relief or BP <100), morphine (1-5mg IV titration), and beta blockers to maintain HR <60 (if no heart block).

cards.front: What dual anti-platelet therapy is used in non-STEMI?
cards.back: Aspirin (whole tablet chewed, then 100mg daily indefinitely) and clopidogrel for minimum 12 months. At 12 months, clopidogrel is stopped but aspirin continues for life.

cards.front: What is glycoprotein 2B3A receptor antagonist used for in non-STEMI?
cards.back: Glycoprotein 2B3A receptor antagonist is a potent intravenous antiplatelet drug used selectively in the CCU or as bailout during angioplasty/stenting in non-STEMI patients.

cards.front: What is the troponin timeline in non-STEMI?
cards.back: Troponin levels are checked at admission (may be normal early), then repeated at 3 hours and 6 hours after first patient contact. If non-STEMI, troponin levels rise over time.

cards.front: What determines high-risk status in non-STEMI regarding troponins?
cards.back: Positive troponin elevation is a marker of high risk in non-STEMI. These patients proceed to angiography for revascularization.

cards.front: How long should serial troponins be checked in low-risk non-STEMI?
cards.back: Serial troponins (3-4 measurements) should all be normal with no further pain or ECG changes to confirm low-risk status. Then stress testing is performed to decide on further imaging or drug therapy.

cards.front: What is the spectrum of presentation from low to high risk based on chest pain location?
cards.back: Low-risk: very localized anterior chest pain or back/interscapular pain. Higher risk: central chest pain or epigastric pain. Highest risk: anterior chest pain radiating to both arms, epigastrium, throat, jaw, or resuscitated cardiac arrest/cardiogenic shock.

cards.front: What does troponin negativity at multiple timepoints suggest?
cards.back: Negative troponins at initial check, 3 hours, and 6 hours rules out acute myocardial infarction and may suggest a non-cardiac cause of chest pain.

cards.front: What is the management approach when ST elevation is detected on ECG?
cards.back: When ST elevation is detected, it indicates STEMI and is an emergency. Do not wait for troponin levels. Proceed immediately with primary PCI or fibrinolytic therapy with dual antiplatelet therapy.

cards.front: What discharge medications are given to all patients with acute coronary syndrome?
cards.back: All ACS patients receive dual antiplatelet therapy for 1 year (aspirin indefinitely), lipid-lowering therapy, ACE inhibitors if EF <40%, and aggressive risk factor management.

cards.front: What are the basic steps of ECG interpretation?
What is the ECG paper speed and time calibration?
cards.back: Paper speed: 25 mm/second. X-axis (time): each 1 mm = 0.04 seconds (40 milliseconds). Y-axis (amplitude): each 10 mm = 1 millivolt.

cards.front: How many electrodes and leads are used in a standard ECG?
cards.back: 10 electrodes that produce 12 leads: 3 standard limb leads (I, II, III), 3 augmented limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF), and 6 precordial leads (V1-V6).

cards.front: What are the locations of precordial ECG leads?
cards.back: V1: 4th intercostal space, right sternal border. V2: 4th intercostal space, left sternal border. V3: Between V2 and V4. V4: 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line. V5: Between V4 and V6. V6: 5th intercostal space, midaxillary line.

cards.front: Which ECG leads view which cardiac walls?
cards.back: Inferior leads (II, III, aVF): inferior wall. Lateral leads (I, aVL, V5, V6): lateral wall. Anterior leads (V1-V4): anterior wall and septum. V1-V2: septal region.

cards.front: How is heart rate calculated from an ECG?
cards.back: Method 1: Count QRS complexes in 10 seconds and multiply by 6. Method 2 (regular rhythm): Count large blocks between two R waves and divide into 300.

cards.front: What defines normal sinus rhythm?
cards.back: P wave before every QRS complex and QRS after every P wave. Rate 60-100 bpm. P wave is negative in aVR and positive in lead II (normal pattern).

cards.front: What is the impulse conduction pathway in the heart?
cards.back: SA node → intra-atrial pathways → AV node (with delay) → bundle of His → right and left bundle branches → Purkinje system → ventricular depolarization.

cards.front: What causes sinus bradycardia and what is the heart rate?
cards.back: Heart rate <60 bpm. Causes: beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, athletes, right coronary artery ischemia/MI (which supplies SA and AV nodes), hypothyroidism, hypothermia, increased intracranial pressure.

cards.front: What is sinus tachycardia and its causes?
cards.back: Heart rate 100-150 bpm in sinus rhythm. Physiological causes: anxiety, pain, fever, exercise. Drug-related: adrenaline, atropine, caffeine, alcohol, salbutamol. Pathological: anemia, hypovolemia, hyperthyroidism.

cards.front: What is the normal cardiac axis and its deviations?
cards.back: Normal axis: -30 to +90 degrees. Left axis deviation (LAD): -30 to -90 degrees. Right axis deviation (RAD): +90 to +180 degrees.

cards.front: What are the ECG findings in left axis deviation?
cards.back: Positive R-wave in lead I, negative R-wave (or small R) in aVF and lead II. Causes: Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome, inferior MI, left ventricular hypertrophy.

cards.front: What are the ECG findings in right axis deviation?
cards.back: Negative R-wave in lead I, positive R-wave in aVF. Causes: right ventricular hypertrophy, certain types of WPW syndrome, atrial fibrillation, hyperkalemia, atrial septal defect.

cards.front: What is P-pulmonale and what does it indicate?
cards.back: P-pulmonale: P-waves >2.5 mm tall (peaked). Indicates right atrial enlargement. Associated with pulmonary hypertension, pulmonary stenosis, tricuspid stenosis.

cards.front: What is P-mitrale and what does it indicate?
cards.back: P-mitrale: P-waves >120 milliseconds wide with bifid (two-peaked) appearance. Indicates left atrial enlargement. Associated with mitral valve disease.

cards.front: What is the PR interval and what is the normal duration?
cards.back: PR interval: measured from start of P-wave to start of R-wave. Represents time of atrial depolarization plus AV node conduction delay. Normal: 120-200 milliseconds. Should be consistent beat-to-beat.

cards.front: How are Q-waves classified as pathological?
cards.back: Q-waves are pathological if: (1) >25% height of following R-wave, OR (2) >1 small block (40 ms) in width. Pathological Q-waves usually indicate previous MI but can also occur with LVH and bundle branch blocks.

cards.front: What is the normal QRS duration and R-wave progression?
cards.back: QRS complex: <120 milliseconds (less than 3 small blocks). R-wave should increase progressively in height from V1 to V6 (normal R-wave progression). This is because left ventricle has the most mass.

cards.front: What are the criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) on ECG?
cards.back: LVH criteria: (1) R-wave in V5 or V6 >27 mm, OR (2) S-wave in V1 + R-wave in V5 >35 mm. Also shows deeper S-wave in V1-V2 and taller R-wave in V5-V6.

cards.front: What are the ECG findings in right ventricular hypertrophy (RVH)?
cards.back: RVH shows: (1) Dominant R-wave in V1 (R-wave taller than S-wave), (2) Persistent S-wave in V5-V6. Causes: chronic lung disease, pulmonary hypertension.

cards.front: What causes QRS widening in bundle branch blocks?
cards.back: Bundle branch blocks cause QRS >120 milliseconds because impulses cannot travel through the blocked bundle and must spread myocyte-to-myocyte through myocardium, which is much slower than normal conduction system.

cards.front: What are the ECG patterns in right bundle branch block (RBBB)?
cards.back: RBBB shows: (1) QRS >120 ms, (2) M-pattern (RSR') in V1-V2, (3) W-pattern in V6, (4) Right ventricle depolarizes after left ventricle.

cards.front: What are the ECG patterns in left bundle branch block (LBBB)?
cards.back: LBBB shows: (1) QRS >120 ms, (2) W-pattern in V1-V2, (3) M-pattern (RSR') in V6, (4) Left ventricle depolarizes after right ventricle.

cards.front: What is the J-point and ST segment elevation significance?
cards.back: J-point: junction where QRS complex meets the isoelectric line. ST segment elevation >1 mm from the J-point is significant. Main causes: STEMI (most common), pericarditis, LV aneurysm, Prinzmetal angina, LBB, Brugada syndrome.

cards.front: How does pericarditis differ from STEMI on ECG?
cards.back: Pericarditis: (1) Saddle-shaped (concave) ST elevation, (2) No Q-waves, (3) No reciprocal ST depression, (4) Widespread ST elevation over most leads, (5) PR segment depression in II, (6) PR segment elevation in aVR (pathognomonic).

cards.front: What is reciprocal ST change in myocardial infarction?
cards.back: Reciprocal changes: ST elevation in one area of the heart is accompanied by ST depression in another (opposite) area. Occurs in STEMI but NOT in pericarditis. Indicates a single affected territory.

cards.front: What is ST depression and what causes it?
cards.back: ST depression: >0.5 mm below baseline. Causes: myocardial ischemia/strain, digoxin (downsloping with T-wave inversion called reverse tick), hypokalemia.

cards.front: What are the stages of myocardial infarction on ECG?
cards.back: Stage 1: Peaked/hyperacute T-waves. Stage 2: ST segment elevation. Stage 3: Q-wave formation (necrosis). Stage 4: ST returns to normal, T-waves invert. Stage 5: Q-waves persist as marker of previous MI.

cards.front: Which ECG leads correspond to which MI territories?
cards.back: Anterior MI: V1-V4. Lateral MI: I, aVL, V5-V6. Anterolateral MI: V1-V5, I, aVL. Inferior MI: II, III, aVF. Anteroseptal: V1-V3. Infrolateral: II, III, aVF, V5-V6.

cards.front: How do you diagnose right ventricular infarction?
cards.back: Right ventricular MI usually occurs with inferior MI. Place V4 lead on right side of chest (called V4R) and look for ST elevation. ST elevation in V4R indicates RV infarction.

cards.front: How do you diagnose posterior myocardial infarction on ECG?
cards.back: Posterior MI usually accompanies inferior MI. Signs: (1) Tall R-wave (reciprocal Q-wave) in V1 with ST depression, (2) Can place precordial leads on back of chest (V7, V8, V9) and look for ST elevation.

cards.front: What are the ECG findings in hypokalemia?
cards.back: Hypokalemia shows: (1) Flattened T-waves, (2) Prominent U-wave, (3) ST depression, (4) T-wave inversion, (5) Downsloping ST segment similar to digoxin effect.

cards.front: What are the ECG findings in hyperkalemia?
cards.back: Early hyperkalemia: (1) Peaked (tall, narrow) T-waves. As severity increases: (2) Flattened P-waves, (3) Prolonged PR interval, (4) Narrower, broader QRS complex (Splinterung), (5) Can progress to ventricular fibrillation (medical emergency).

cards.front: What are the ECG findings in hypothermia?
cards.back: Hypothermia shows: (1) Bradycardia, (2) Irregular baseline (from shivering), (3) J-wave or Osborn wave at end of QRS complex before ST segment.

cards.front: What is first-degree AV block?
cards.back: First-degree AV block: (1) Prolonged PR interval >200 ms (>5 small blocks), (2) Constant PR duration, (3) Every P-wave followed by QRS complex (1:1 conduction). Causes: ischemia, hypokalemia, rheumatic heart disease, aortic root abscess, digoxin, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers.

cards.front: What is Mobitz type 1 (Wenckebach) second-degree AV block?
cards.back: Mobitz type 1: (1) Progressive prolongation of PR interval beat-to-beat (lengthening), (2) Then sudden P-wave without QRS (dropped beat), (3) Cycle repeats. Most PR intervals are conducted. Usually benign.

cards.front: What is Mobitz type 2 second-degree AV block?
cards.back: Mobitz type 2: (1) Constant PR interval (no lengthening), (2) Sudden dropped beats (P-wave without QRS), (3) Regular pattern of block (e.g., 2:1, 3:1). More serious than Mobitz I, requires pacemaker insertion due to risk of complete heart block.

cards.front: How do you differentiate Mobitz type 1 and type 2 AV block?
cards.back: Mobitz type 1: PR interval progressively lengthens before dropped beat. Mobitz type 2: PR interval remains constant, no progressive lengthening.

cards.front: What is third-degree AV block (complete heart block)?
cards.back: Third-degree AV block: (1) No relationship between P-waves and QRS complexes, (2) P-waves and PP intervals are regular (atrial rate from SA node), (3) QRS complexes are regular at slower rate (ventricular escape rhythm), (4) Broad QRS complex from ventricular origin, (5) Complete AV dissociation. Requires pacemaker insertion.

cards.front: What approach should be used for tachyarrhythmias?
cards.back: Approach: (1) Identify rhythm strip (usually lead II), (2) Determine if regular or irregular, (3) Determine if narrow or broad QRS, (4) Narrow complex = always supraventricular tachycardia, (5) Broad complex = 80% ventricular tachycardia, 20% SVT with BBB.

cards.front: What is atrial fibrillation and its ECG appearance?
cards.back: Atrial fibrillation: (1) Rapid chaotic depolarization from multiple foci, (2) Absent P-waves with fibrillating baseline, (3) Irregular (irregularly irregular) QRS complexes, (4) Ventricular rate variable. Most common arrhythmia.

cards.front: What is atrial flutter and its ECG appearance?
cards.back: Atrial flutter: (1) Reentry circuit around right atrium, (2) Atrial rate 250-350 bpm, (3) Typical sawtooth flutter waves on baseline, (4) Regular QRS complexes (usually 2:1, 3:1, or 4:1 AV conduction), (5) AV node cannot conduct all atrial impulses.

cards.front: What is atrial tachycardia and its characteristics?
cards.back: Atrial tachycardia: (1) Heart rate often >150 bpm, (2) P-waves present but may not be easily visible, (3) Abnormally shaped P-waves (may appear just after QRS), (4) Due to ectopic focus from abnormal area of atrium.

cards.front: What is ventricular tachycardia and how is it defined?
cards.back: Ventricular tachycardia: (1) Broad QRS complex (>120 ms), (2) Rate >120 bpm, (3) Three or more successive ventricular beats, (4) Arises from reentry circuit or ectopic ventricular focus (e.g., scar from previous MI), (5) May be self-terminating or sustained, (6) Can degenerate to ventricular fibrillation.

cards.front: What are the types of ventricular tachycardia?
cards.back: Monomorphic VT: All QRS complexes look the same (from single focus). Polymorphic VT: Varying QRS complexes. Torsades de Pointes: Type of polymorphic VT with twisting pattern, caused by prolonged QT interval from electrolyte abnormalities or drugs.

cards.front: What causes accelerated ventricular rhythm and idioventricular rhythm?
cards.back: Accelerated ventricular rhythm: Heart rate 40-120 bpm (between normal escape rate and VT). Idioventricular rhythm: Heart rate 30-40 bpm (ventricular escape rhythm). Both are slower than VT (>120 bpm).

cards.front: What is ventricular fibrillation and how is it managed?
cards.back: Ventricular fibrillation: (1) Rapidly fatal arrhythmia, (2) Most common cause is myocardial infarction, (3) Can occur with electrolyte abnormalities, (4) Requires urgent defibrillation (electrical cardioversion).

cards.front: What is asystole?
cards.back: Asystole: Absence of any electrical cardiac activity on ECG (flat line). Results in no cardiac output and requires urgent CPR and ACLS protocol.

cards.front: What is torsades de pointes?
cards.back: Torsades de Pointes: Polymorphic VT with twisting pattern around the baseline. Caused by prolonged QT interval from electrolyte abnormalities (hypomagnesemia) or QT-prolonging drugs (antiarrhythmics). Often associated with sudden syncope and VF. Treated with IV magnesium and electrical cardioversion.

cards.front: What is Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome?
cards.back: WPW syndrome: (1) Accessory pathway (bundle of Kent) between atrium and ventricles, (2) Bypass of AV node causes short PR interval (<120 ms), (3) Delta wave (slurred upstroke) in QRS complex, (4) Can develop reentry tachycardia (AVNRT circuit).

cards.front: What is the delta wave in WPW syndrome?
cards.back: Delta wave: Slurred upstroke at the beginning of QRS complex in WPW syndrome. Caused by slow myocyte-to-myocyte depolarization through accessory pathway (not through normal conduction system).

cards.front: How does the accessory pathway in WPW cause short PR interval?
cards.back: In WPW: (1) Accessory pathway bypasses the AV node delay, (2) Conduction through accessory pathway is faster than through AV node, (3) Ventricles depolarize immediately after atrium, (4) Results in short PR interval (<120 ms).

cards.front: What is AV reentry tachycardia (AVNRT) in WPW?
cards.back: AVNRT in WPW: (1) Macro reentry circuit develops, (2) Impulse travels down AV node through bundle branches into myocardium, (3) Then back up through accessory pathway, (4) Depolarizes atrium and restarts circuit, (5) Results in rapid narrow or broad complex tachycardia.

cards.front: What are the differential diagnoses for narrow regular tachycardia?
cards.back: Narrow regular tachycardia (SVT): (1) Sinus tachycardia, (2) Atrial flutter, (3) Atrial tachycardia, (4) AV nodal reentry tachycardia, (5) AV reentry tachycardia (WPW).

cards.front: What are the differential diagnoses for narrow irregular tachycardia?
cards.back: Narrow irregular tachycardia: (1) Atrial fibrillation (most common), (2) Atrial flutter with variable AV block, (3) Atrial tachycardia with variable block.

cards.front: What are the differential diagnoses for wide regular tachycardia?
cards.back: Wide regular tachycardia: (1) Ventricular tachycardia (80%), (2) SVT with bundle branch block (20%).

cards.front: What are the differential diagnoses for wide irregular tachycardia?
cards.back: Wide irregular tachycardia: (1) Atrial fibrillation with bundle branch block, (2) Atrial fibrillation with WPW syndrome, (3) Polymorphic VT/Torsades de Pointes.

cards.front: What is rheumatic fever by definition?
cards.back: A systemic disease of childhood, often recurrent, that follows group A beta-hemolytic streptococcal infection. It is a diffuse inflammatory disease of connective tissue affecting the heart, blood vessels, joints, subcutaneous tissue, and central nervous system.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the pathogenesis of rheumatic fever?
cards.back: Delayed immune response to infection with group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus. Typically has a latent period of 1-3 weeks, followed by antibody-induced immunological damage to heart valves, joints, subcutaneous tissue, and basal ganglia.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What are the major criteria of the modified Jones criteria for rheumatic fever diagnosis?
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What are the minor criteria of the modified Jones criteria for rheumatic fever?
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What supporting evidence is used to confirm streptococcal infection in rheumatic fever?
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the treatment of choice for primary prevention of rheumatic fever?
cards.back: Benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units intramuscularly in a single dose, OR Penicillin V (pen VK) 500mg orally twice daily for 10 days. For penicillin-allergic patients: Erythromycin for 5-10 days.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What are the anti-inflammatory agents used in rheumatic fever treatment?
cards.back: For arthritis: Aspirin for rapid resolution of fever and arthralgia (but does not decrease incidence of rheumatic heart disease). For carditis: Prednisone 2-2.5 mg/kg twice daily for 2 weeks, then taper over 2 weeks while introducing aspirin. Reserved for acute severe carditis.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the duration of secondary prophylaxis for rheumatic fever with carditis and residual heart disease?
cards.back: Prophylaxis for at least 10 years since the last attack and at least until age 40. Sometimes lifelong prophylaxis is used. High-risk patients may benefit from administration every 3 weeks instead of every 4 weeks.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the duration of secondary prophylaxis for rheumatic fever with carditis but no residual heart disease?
cards.back: Prophylaxis for 10 years or until age 21, whichever is longer.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the duration of secondary prophylaxis for rheumatic fever without carditis?
cards.back: Prophylaxis for 5 years or until age 21, whichever is longer.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What are the predominant symptoms of valvular heart disease?
cards.back: Dyspnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND), orthopnea, palpitations, syncope, presyncope, cough, hemoptysis, and chest pain.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What causes dyspnea in valvular heart disease?
cards.back: Pulmonary congestion due to heart failure, which causes interstitial and alveolar edema. This stimulates J receptors in the lungs, which stimulates respiration.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the difference between central and peripheral cyanosis in valve disease?
cards.back: Central cyanosis: Due to reduced oxygenation from right-to-left shunting or impaired pulmonary function. Peripheral cyanosis: Due to cutaneous vasoconstriction or reduced cardiac output.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What are the common causes of palpitations in valvular heart disease?
cards.back: Arrhythmias, heart block, ectopic beats, high output states, and sudden onset bradycardias.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What are the common causes of bilateral lower limb edema?
cards.back: Bilateral edema most commonly indicates heart failure, but other causes include renal causes and hepatic causes.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What are the physical examination findings to look for in valvular disease?
cards.back: General exam for anemia, clubbing, jaundice, splinter hemorrhages, and peripheral features of valvular heart disease.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What are the common causes of valvular heart disease?
cards.back: In developing countries: Rheumatic heart disease (most common). Other causes: Congenital lesions, degeneration or calcification with age, infective endocarditis, connective tissue disease, collagen vascular disease, and drugs.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What are the goals of medical management in valvular heart disease?
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the most common cause of mitral stenosis?
cards.back: Rheumatic fever, which causes fusion of commissures, cusps, and chordae.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What are the rarer causes of mitral stenosis?
cards.back: Congenital, carcinoid syndrome, SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, and amyloid.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the typical presentation of mitral stenosis?
cards.back: Exertional dyspnea (due to reduced pulmonary compliance), palpitations (often from atrial fibrillation), hemoptysis, and hoarseness of voice.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the character of the pulse in mitral stenosis?
cards.back: Small volume pulse. May be irregularly irregular if the patient has developed atrial fibrillation.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the character of the apex beat in mitral stenosis?
cards.back: Tapping and non-displaced.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is a diastolic thrill in mitral stenosis?
cards.back: A palpable vibration at the apex during diastole due to turbulent flow across the stenosed mitral valve.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What does a palpable P2 and loud P2 indicate in mitral stenosis?
cards.back: Features of pulmonary hypertension. P2 is found in the second left intercostal space.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What does a parasternal heave indicate in mitral stenosis?
cards.back: Left atrial or right ventricular enlargement.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the first heart sound in mitral stenosis?
cards.back: Usually loud due to the unopened, calcified valve. May be soft if the valve is heavily calcified.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: What is the pathophysiological basis for the loud first heart sound in mitral stenosis?
cards.back: The mitral valve is prevented from moving in its normal range, remaining in a more open position until ventricular systole occurs, causing the valve to slam shut with a loud sound.
cards.source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009
cards.sourceFile: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml

cards.front: According to 2019 WHO statistics, what is the leading cause of global mortality, accounting for 18.5 million deaths annually?
cards.back: Cardiovascular disease.

cards.front: Individually, what percentage of total global deaths is attributed to coronary artery disease (CAD)?
cards.back: 16% of total deaths.

cards.front: What term is now preferred for what was previously categorized as 'stable angina'?
cards.back: Chronic coronary syndrome.

cards.front: At what age does the prevalence of coronary artery disease typically begin to increase significantly?
cards.back: After the age of 35 years.

cards.front: What is the lifetime risk of a man developing CAD after the age of 40?
What is the lifetime risk of a woman developing CAD after the age of 40?
How does the risk of CAD in women change after menopause?
cards.back: The prevalence becomes equivalent to that of men.

cards.front: What specific family history criteria define an additional risk factor for CAD in a male patient?
cards.back: A father or brother diagnosed with CAD before age 55.

cards.front: What specific family history criteria define an additional risk factor for CAD in a female patient?
cards.back: A mother or sister diagnosed with CAD before age 65.

cards.front: Compared to non-diabetics, how much does diabetes mellitus increase the rate of coronary artery disease?
cards.back: By more than 2.5 times.

cards.front: Non-smokers exposed to secondhand smoke face a _____% to _____% increased risk of CAD.
What dietary intake is associated with a 22% higher risk of myocardial infarction?
cards.back: Added sugar (specifically soft drinks and sweetened beverages).

cards.front: Which inflammatory conditions are recognized as less common risk factors for CAD development?
cards.back: Systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, and HIV.

cards.front: What are the components used to calculate the Framingham 10-year risk assessment score?
cards.back: Age, total cholesterol, HDL-cholesterol, systolic blood pressure, and smoking status.

cards.front: A patient with total cholesterol exceeding _____ $mmol/L$ or LDL exceeding _____ $mmol/L$ is automatically categorized as very high risk.
What GFR threshold defines 'Stage 4' Chronic Kidney Disease, placing a patient at very high risk for CAD?
cards.back: A GFR less than 30 $ml/min/1.73m^2$.

cards.front: What is the earliest pathological marking of coronary artery disease often seen in the teen years?
cards.back: The fatty streak.

cards.front: Clinical manifestations of ischemic heart disease typically occur only once a coronary vessel is narrowed by what percentage?
What is the defining pathological event that transitions stable CAD to an Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)?
cards.back: Plaque rupture followed by thrombus formation.

cards.front: In Acute Coronary Syndrome, what distinguishes a STEMI from a Non-STEMI pathologically?
cards.back: STEMI involves total vessel occlusion by thrombus, whereas Non-STEMI involves non-total (subtotal) occlusion.

cards.front: What is the definition of stable angina regarding the duration of symptom consistency?
cards.back: Symptom characteristics remain unchanged for the preceding 60 days.

cards.front: What are the three criteria for 'Typical Angina'?
cards.back: Retrosternal discomfort, provoked by exertion/stress, and relieved by rest/nitrates within 5 minutes.

cards.front: According to the Canadian Cardiovascular Society (CCS), what defines Grade III angina?
cards.back: Angina occurring with mild exertion.

cards.front: What defines CCS Grade IV angina?
cards.back: Angina occurring at rest.

cards.front: On an ECG, what measurement constitutes significant ST-segment depression in the limb leads?
cards.back: Any depression in excess of 1 mm (especially if horizontal).

cards.front: What ECG finding is characteristic of an ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) in the chest leads?
cards.back: More than 2 mm of concave ST-segment elevation.

cards.front: What is indicated by the presence of 'pathological Q-waves' on an ECG?
cards.back: A part of the heart has completely infarcted (necrosed).

cards.front: Which ECG leads correspond to an 'Anterior' myocardial infarction?
cards.back: V1 to V4.

cards.front: Which ECG leads correspond to an 'Inferior' myocardial infarction?
cards.back: II, III, and aVF.

cards.front: What is the standard treadmill protocol used for exercise stress testing?
cards.back: The Bruce protocol.

cards.front: What are the primary aims of managing chronic coronary syndrome?
cards.back: To prevent myocardial infarction, delay death, and improve quality of life/symptoms.

cards.front: Which two anti-platelet drugs are commonly used in the setting of coronary artery disease?
cards.back: Aspirin and Clopidogrel.

cards.front: What is the pharmacological mechanism of Clopidogrel?
cards.back: It is a P2Y12 (ADP) receptor antagonist.

cards.front: What is the resting heart rate target for a patient on beta-blocker therapy for angina?
cards.back: Below 60 beats per minute (ideally in the 50s).

cards.front: What is the LDL cholesterol target for patients with established coronary artery disease?
cards.back: Less than 1.4 $mmol/L$.

cards.front: For diabetic patients with CAD, what is the target $HbA_{1c}$ level?
cards.back: Less than 7%.

cards.front: What are the recommended exercise guidelines for CAD lifestyle modification?
cards.back: 30 minutes of endurance training 5 days a week, plus resistance training.

cards.front: What is the 'total ischemic time' goal for a patient presenting with a STEMI?
cards.back: Less than 3 hours from symptom onset to vessel opening.

cards.front: A diagnosis of STEMI in the acute setting is based on what two findings?
cards.back: Typical angina ongoing for $> 30$ minutes and ECG evidence of ST elevation.

cards.front: Why is it critical not to wait for troponin results in a suspected STEMI case?
cards.back: Laboratory results take too long, and STEMI is a time-dependent emergency requiring immediate reperfusion.

cards.front: Which fibrinolytic agent is preferred because it is administered as a single IV bolus?
cards.back: Tenecteplase.

cards.front: What is the loading dose and duration for Clopidogrel in the treatment of a STEMI?
cards.back: 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg daily for one year.

cards.front: Which anticoagulant is generally preferred over heparin in STEMI management due to ease of administration?
cards.back: Enoxaparin (given subcutaneously).

cards.front: What is the standard high-dose statin therapy initiated in the acute setting of a myocardial infarction?
cards.back: Atorvastatin 80 mg.

cards.front: What are three major absolute contraindications to fibrinolysis?
cards.back: Previous hemorrhagic stroke, active internal bleeding, and suspected aortic dissection.

cards.front: What is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death during the early stages of a myocardial infarction?
cards.back: Ventricular tachycardia (VT) or ventricular fibrillation (VF).

cards.front: What defines the difference between a Non-STEMI (NSTEMI) and Unstable Angina?
cards.back: NSTEMI has elevated biomarkers of necrosis (Troponin positive), while Unstable Angina does not (Troponin negative).

cards.front: What heart rate defines sinus bradycardia?
cards.back: Less than 60 beats per minute.

cards.front: What is the normal range for a PR interval on an ECG?
cards.back: 120 to 200 milliseconds (0.12 to 0.2 seconds).

cards.front: What defines 'P-pulmonale' on an ECG?
cards.back: A tall P-wave ($> 2.5$ mm) indicating right atrial enlargement.

cards.front: What defines 'P-mitrale' on an ECG?
cards.back: A wide, bifid P-wave ($> 120$ ms) indicating left atrial enlargement.

cards.front: What ECG pattern is characteristic of a Right Bundle Branch Block (RBBB)?
cards.back: An M-pattern in lead V1 and a W-pattern in lead V6.

cards.front: What ECG pattern is characteristic of a Left Bundle Branch Block (LBBB)?
cards.back: A W-pattern in lead V1 and an M-pattern in lead V6.

cards.front: What is the pathognomonic ECG finding for pericarditis?
cards.back: Widespread concave (saddle-shaped) ST elevation and PR segment depression (except in aVR).

cards.front: What are the ECG signs of hyperkalemia?
cards.back: Tall peaked T-waves, flattened P-waves, and prolonged PR interval, eventually leading to broad QRS complexes.

cards.front: What ECG feature is seen in hypothermia?
cards.back: An Osborne wave (J-wave) at the end of the QRS complex.

cards.front: What defines a Second-Degree Mobitz Type I (Wenckebach) heart block?
cards.back: Progressive prolongation of the PR interval until a QRS complex is dropped.

cards.front: What defines a Second-Degree Mobitz Type II heart block?
cards.back: A constant PR interval with intermittent, sudden dropped QRS complexes.

cards.front: What are the two hallmarks of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) on an ECG?
cards.back: Absence of P-waves and an 'irregularly irregular' R-R interval.

cards.front: What is the characteristic baseline pattern seen in Atrial Flutter?
cards.back: A 'sawtooth' pattern of flutter waves.

cards.front: What are the two classic ECG features of Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome?
cards.back: A short PR interval and a Delta wave (slurred upstroke of the QRS).

cards.front: What is the causal agent of Rheumatic Fever?
cards.back: Group A beta-hemolytic streptococci.

cards.front: What are the five 'Major' Jones Criteria for diagnosing Rheumatic Fever?
cards.back: Carditis, Polyarthritis, Sydenham's chorea, Erythema marginatum, and Subcutaneous nodules.

cards.front: What is the treatment of choice for primary prevention (eradication) of streptococcal infection in rheumatic fever?
cards.back: Benzathine penicillin G (intramuscular).

cards.front: How long should secondary prophylaxis be continued for a patient with rheumatic fever and residual heart disease?
cards.back: At least 10 years since the last episode and until at least age 40 (sometimes lifelong).

cards.front: What is the characteristic apex beat finding in Mitral Stenosis?
cards.back: A tapping, non-displaced apex beat.

cards.front: What is the classic auscultatory triad of Mitral Stenosis?
cards.back: Loud first heart sound ($S_1$), an opening snap, and a mid-diastolic rumbling murmur.

cards.front: What valve area measurement indicates 'severe' Mitral Stenosis?
cards.back: Less than 1.0 $cm^2$.

cards.front: What type of murmur is associated with Mitral Regurgitation?
cards.back: A blowing, high-pitched pansystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla.

cards.front: In Mitral Regurgitation, what end-systolic diameter (ESD) threshold on Echo suggests a need for surgery in asymptomatic patients?
cards.back: ESD $> 40$ mm (or $> 45$ mm according to textbook sections).

cards.front: What are the three cardinal symptoms of Aortic Stenosis?
cards.back: Angina, syncope, and dyspnea.

cards.front: What is the characteristic pulse associated with Aortic Stenosis?
cards.back: Pulsus parvus et tardus (slow-rising and sustained pulse).

cards.front: What pressure gradient on Echo signifies 'severe' Aortic Stenosis?
cards.back: A mean gradient $> 40$ $mmHg$ (or peak gradient $> 64$ $mmHg$).

cards.front: What is the life expectancy of a patient with Aortic Stenosis once heart failure symptoms develop?
cards.back: Approximately 2 years.

cards.front: What are the signs of 'severe' Aortic Regurgitation during physical exam?
cards.back: Widened pulse pressure, collapsing (water-hammer) pulse, and Corrigans pulse (visible carotid pulsations).

cards.front: What is an 'Austin Flint' murmur?
cards.back: A mid-diastolic rumble at the apex heard in severe aortic regurgitation due to rapid flow across the mitral valve.

cards.front: What are the two most common pathogens in 'Acute' versus 'Subacute' Infective Endocarditis?
cards.back: Staphylococcus aureus (Acute) and Streptococcus viridans (Subacute).

cards.front: Which Infective Endocarditis pathogen is most commonly associated with colorectal carcinoma?
cards.back: Streptococcus bovis.

cards.front: What are 'Osler's nodes' and 'Janeway lesions' in the context of Infective Endocarditis?
cards.back: Osler's nodes are painful nodules on digits; Janeway lesions are painless macules on palms/soles.

cards.front: According to the Duke Criteria, what constitutes a 'Major' clinical criterion for IE?
cards.back: Positive blood cultures (typical organism) and evidence of endocardial involvement on Echo (vegetation/abscess).

cards.front: What are the three components of Beck’s Triad for cardiac tamponade?
cards.back: Hypotension, distended jugular veins, and muffled heart sounds.

cards.front: What is 'Pulsus Paradoxus'?
cards.back: An exaggerated drop in systolic blood pressure ($> 10$ $mmHg$) during inspiration, seen in cardiac tamponade.

cards.front: What is the definitive treatment for cardiac tamponade?
cards.back: Pericardiocentesis.

cards.front: What is Kussmaul’s sign, often seen in constrictive pericarditis?
cards.back: A paradoxical rise in JVP during inspiration.

cards.front: What heart sound is characteristic of constrictive pericarditis?
cards.back: A pericardial knock.

cards.front: What is the definitive treatment for constrictive pericarditis?
cards.back: Surgical pericardiectomy.

cards.front: What is the definition of Hypertension based on standard blood pressure readings?
cards.back: Usual blood pressure $> 140/90$ $mmHg$.

cards.front: For high-risk patients (e.g., those with CAD or Diabetes), what is the threshold for initiating hypertension treatment?
cards.back: Blood pressure $> 130/80$ $mmHg$.

cards.front: What constitutes 'Hypertensive Crisis' according to the textbook sections?
cards.back: A diastolic blood pressure $> 140$ $mmHg$.

cards.front: In a patient with ACS, what is the 'loading dose' of Aspirin given initially?
cards.back: 325 mg (stat dose to chew).

cards.front: What echocardiographic finding is used to evaluate for right ventricular infarction in the setting of inferior MI?
cards.back: ST elevation in the right-sided lead V4R.

cards.front: How does the 're-entry circuit' in Atrial Flutter differ from that in Atrial Fibrillation?
cards.back: Flutter is a single organized circuit (usually right atrium), whereas AF involves multiple chaotic foci.

cards.front: What common medication class should be avoided or used with caution in a patient with inferior MI and bradycardia?
cards.back: Beta-blockers.

cards.front: What condition is suggested by a 'waterbottle' appearance of the cardiac shadow on chest X-ray?
cards.back: Pericardial effusion.

cards.front: In Infective Endocarditis, what size of vegetation is considered a surgical indication if it persists after a major embolic event?
cards.back: Greater than 1 cm in diameter.

cards.front: What is infective endocarditis?
cards.back: Infection of the endocardial surface, typically with vegetations on the endocardium. The heart valves are most commonly affected. It is virtually always fatal if untreated.

cards.front: How is infective endocarditis classified?
cards.back: Four categories: 1) Native valve endocarditis 2) Prosthetic valve endocarditis 3) IV drug abuse-related endocarditis 4) Nosocomial endocarditis. Also classified as acute or subacute.

cards.front: What is the difference between acute and subacute infective endocarditis?
cards.back: Acute: marked toxicity, progresses over days to weeks, causes valvular destruction and metastatic infection. Usually caused by Staphylococcus aureus. Fatal in <6 weeks if untreated. Subacute: evolves over weeks to months with modest toxicity. Usually caused by Streptococcus viridans. Takes longer than 6 weeks to be fatal if untreated.

cards.front: What is the annual incidence and epidemiology of infective endocarditis?
cards.back: Annual incidence: 15-20,000 cases per year. Fourth leading cause of life-threatening infectious disease. Slight predilection for males (ratio 1.7:1). Mitral valve affected in up to 45% of cases, aortic valve in 36%.

cards.front: What are the most common etiological agents of infective endocarditis?
cards.back: Streptococcus group: up to 80% of cases. Staphylococcus: 20-35%. Gram-negative aerobic bacilli: up to 13%. Other bacteria and fungi. Culture-negative endocarditis: up to 24% of cases.

cards.front: What are the main predisposing factors for infective endocarditis?
cards.back: Structural heart disease: Rheumatic heart disease (76%), congenital heart disease, degenerative cardiac lesions, prosthetic valves. IV drug use (highest risk in patients <40 years). Approximately 75% of patients have pre-existing structural cardiac abnormality.

cards.front: What is the pathogenesis of infective endocarditis?
cards.back: The valve surface is altered to produce a suitable site for bacterial attachment. Platelets and fibrin deposit, forming sterile vegetation (NBTE). When bacteria reach the site, they colonize and are covered with platelets and fibrin, leading to bacterial multiplication and vegetation growth.

cards.front: What is the rate of prosthetic valve infection after implantation?
cards.back: More than 100,000 heart valves implanted annually in the US. Infective endocarditis develops in 1-4% of valve recipients during the first year post-surgery and in approximately 1% per year thereafter.

cards.front: What is the recurrence rate of endocarditis?
cards.back: Recurrent endocarditis occurs in approximately 4.5% of patients. Other studies report rates ranging from 2.5% to 9%.

cards.front: What are the most common congenital heart lesions associated with endocarditis risk?
cards.back: Bicuspid aortic valves, patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), ventricular septal defect (VSD), coarctation of the aorta, and tetralogy of Fallot.

cards.front: What is the increased risk of endocarditis in mitral valve prolapse with regurgitation?
cards.back: The risk is estimated to be 5-8 times higher than in the normal population.

cards.front: In what percentage of cases is aortic valve disease present in endocarditis?
cards.back: Aortic valve stenosis and regurgitation is present in 12-30% of cases of infective endocarditis.

cards.front: What is the clinical presentation of infective endocarditis?
cards.back: Fever (>95% of cases), weakness, weight loss, night sweats, audible murmurs (>85%), Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, splinter hemorrhages, Roth spots (less common), clubbing (20%), splenomegaly, musculoskeletal manifestations, and major embolic episodes.

cards.front: What are the laboratory findings in infective endocarditis?
cards.back: Normocytic anemia, low serum iron and iron-binding capacity, thrombocytopenia (up to 15%), leukocytosis, elevated ESR, hypergammaglobulinemia, proteinuria, microscopic hematuria, red cell casts, positive rheumatoid factor (50%), circulating immune complexes, positive ANA, elevated serum globulins, cryoglobulins, hypocomplementemia.

cards.front: Why is blood culture the most important laboratory test in endocarditis?
cards.back: Positive cultures are seen in up to 97% of cases. A minimum of three blood cultures should be obtained over a time period based on severity of illness. Blood culture is crucial for organism identification and antibiotic sensitivity.

cards.front: What is the specificity and sensitivity of echocardiography in endocarditis?
cards.back: Transthoracic echo: high specificity of 98%. Transesophageal echo: higher ultrasonic frequencies improve spatial resolution with high specificity and even higher sensitivity.

cards.front: What are the indications for transesophageal echocardiography in suspected endocarditis?
cards.back: 1) Patient has prosthetic valve or intracardiac device 2) Poor quality transthoracic echo 3) Negative transthoracic echo but high clinical suspicion for endocarditis. If transthoracic is negative and clinical suspicion is low, TE is not needed.

cards.front: What are the major criteria for Duke criteria diagnosis of endocarditis?
cards.back: 1) Positive blood culture for typical organisms from 2 separate cultures or persistent positive culture or single positive culture for HACEK group/C. burnetti/S. bovis/S. aureus without primary focus 2) Evidence of endocardial involvement: oscillating intracardiac mass on valve, abscess, new partial dehiscence of prosthetic valve, or new valvular regurgitation.

cards.front: What are the minor criteria for Duke criteria diagnosis of endocarditis?
cards.back: 1) Predisposing heart condition or IV drug use 2) Fever >38°C 3) Vascular phenomena or immunological phenomena (glomerulonephritis, Osler nodes) 4) Echo findings consistent with endocarditis but not meeting major criteria 5) Microbiological evidence not meeting major criteria or serological evidence of active infection.

cards.front: What criteria are required for definite diagnosis of infective endocarditis?
cards.back: Either 2 major criteria OR 1 major and 3 minor criteria OR 5 minor criteria. For possible diagnosis: 1 major and 1 minor OR 3 minor criteria.

cards.front: What is the recommended approach when blood cultures are negative at 48 hours?
cards.back: If clinical or echo evidence of endocarditis persists, consult with microbiology team and consider extended culture or alternative organisms. If no clinical evidence, consider alternative diagnosis.

cards.front: What defines an infected mass in endocarditis at surgery vs on echo?
cards.back: At surgery: infected mass attached to endocardial structure or implanted intracardiac device. On echo: oscillating or non-oscillating intracardiac mass on valve.

cards.front: What is an abscess in infective endocarditis?
cards.back: At surgery/necropsy: perivolvular cavity with necrosis and perivolvular material. On echo: thick and non-homogenous perivolvular area with echo-dense or echo-lucent appearance.

cards.front: What is a fistula in endocarditis?
cards.back: Communication between two neighboring cavities. Identified on echo using color Doppler as communication between two cavities through perforation.

cards.front: What are valvular aneurysms in endocarditis?
cards.back: Outpouching or bulging of valvular tissue. Seen in prosthetic valve endocarditis where there may be dehiscence of the prosthesis, identified on echo as rocking motion of the prosthesis.

cards.front: What are poor prognostic signs in infective endocarditis?
cards.back: Staphylococcus aureus infection, fungal infections, previous endocarditis, cyanotic congenital heart disease, congestive cardiac failure, embolic phenomena, rupture of mycotic aneurysm, lack of response to antibiotics, prosthetic valve endocarditis, periannular extension of infection.

cards.front: What are the poor prognostic patient characteristics in endocarditis?
cards.back: Elderly patients, prosthetic valve, insulin-dependent diabetic patients, patients with multiple comorbidities.

cards.front: What are the echo features suggestive of severe endocarditis?
cards.back: Severe left-sided valve regurgitations, very low ejection fraction, pulmonary hypertension, large vegetations, severe prosthetic valve dysfunction.

cards.front: What is the empirical antibiotic therapy for native valve endocarditis?
cards.back: Penicillin-based antibiotic (ampicillin or amoxicillin-clavulanic acid) plus gentamicin. In penicillin-allergic patients: vancomycin plus gentamicin with ciprofloxacin.

cards.front: What is the antibiotic therapy for early prosthetic valve endocarditis (<1 year post-surgery)?
cards.back: Vancomycin with gentamicin and rifampin at specified doses.

cards.front: What is the antibiotic therapy for late prosthetic valve endocarditis (>1 year post-surgery)?
cards.back: Same as native valve endocarditis (penicillin-based antibiotic with gentamicin or vancomycin-based regimen in allergic patients).

cards.front: What are the absolute indications for surgical therapy in endocarditis?
