cards.back: 1) Refractory heart failure related to valve dysfunction 2) Physiologically significant valve dysfunction on echo 3) More than two serious systemic embolic episodes with large vegetations 4) Persistent/uncontrolled infection despite antibiotics.

cards.front: What are the local complications requiring surgery in endocarditis?
cards.back: Perivolvular myocardial abscesses, mycotic aneurysms, periannular extension of infection.

cards.front: What are the relative indications for surgery in endocarditis?
cards.back: Evidence of perivolvular infection (intracardiac abscess or fistula), rupture of sinus of Valsalva aneurysm, fungal endocarditis, highly resistant microorganism, relapse after adequate antibiotics (especially prosthetic valve), culture-negative endocarditis with fever >10 days after empiric therapy.

cards.front: What echo features are indications for surgical treatment?
cards.back: Persistent vegetation after major embolic episode, vegetation >1cm in diameter, increased vegetation size despite 4 weeks of antibiotics, acute mitral insufficiency, valve perforation or rupture, any peri-annular extension of infection.

cards.front: What is the mortality rate for surgical treatment of active endocarditis?
cards.back: In a study of 138 patients who underwent surgery with active infection, early mortality was 11.5%. Risk factors for early mortality: NYHA class 4, septic shock, advanced age, pre-operative acute renal failure, staphylococcal infection.

cards.front: What are the neurological complications of endocarditis?
cards.back: Embolic phenomena resulting in intracranial hemorrhage, coma, stroke. Patients with heart failure, uncontrolled infection, and absence of high embolic risk should be managed conservatively. Otherwise, surgery must be considered.

cards.front: What is the rationale for endocarditis prophylaxis?
cards.back: Endocarditis usually follows bacteremia. Although risk of endocarditis is small and there is no proof of efficacy of preventative therapy, prophylaxis is recommended for patients with predisposing cardiac lesions undergoing procedures known to cause bacteremia.

cards.front: Which patients are candidates for antibiotic prophylaxis?
cards.back: Patients with valvular heart disease, congenital heart disease, intracardiac prosthesis, or previous history of endocarditis who are undergoing procedures at highest risk for bacteremia.

cards.front: Which procedures require antibiotic prophylaxis for endocarditis prevention?
cards.back: High-risk dental procedures. Prophylaxis is NOT recommended for respiratory tract procedures, GI procedures, or skin and soft tissue procedures. Good dental hygiene and regular dental review are more important than prophylaxis.

cards.front: What is the recommended antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures in non-allergic patients?
cards.back: Amoxicillin or ampicillin 2 grams oral or IV in adults. In children 50 mg/kg oral or IV.

cards.front: What is the recommended antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures in penicillin-allergic patients?
cards.back: Clindamycin 2 grams oral or IV in adults. In children 20 mg/kg oral or IV.

cards.front: Why are aseptic measures during venous catheterization important?
cards.back: Aseptic measures during venous catheterization and invasive procedures are mandatory to prevent bacteremia and subsequent development of nosocomial endocarditis.

atoms.atom: What are the basic steps of ECG interpretation?
atoms.definition:
1. Verify ECG calibration
2. Analyze rate and rhythm
3. Determine cardiac axis
4. Examine each wave morphology
5. Look for chamber dilatation and hypertrophy
6. Assess for myocardial infarction stages
7. Check for metabolic abnormalities

atoms.atom: What is the ECG paper speed and time calibration?
atoms.definition: Paper speed: 25 mm/second. X-axis (time): each 1 mm = 0.04 seconds (40 milliseconds). Y-axis (amplitude): each 10 mm = 1 millivolt.

atoms.atom: How many electrodes and leads are used in a standard ECG?
atoms.definition: 10 electrodes that produce 12 leads: 3 standard limb leads (I, II, III), 3 augmented limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF), and 6 precordial leads (V1-V6).

atoms.atom: What are the locations of precordial ECG leads?
atoms.definition: V1: 4th intercostal space, right sternal border. V2: 4th intercostal space, left sternal border. V3: Between V2 and V4. V4: 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line. V5: Between V4 and V6. V6: 5th intercostal space, midaxillary line.

atoms.atom: Which ECG leads view which cardiac walls?
atoms.definition: Inferior leads (II, III, aVF): inferior wall. Lateral leads (I, aVL, V5, V6): lateral wall. Anterior leads (V1-V4): anterior wall and septum. V1-V2: septal region.

atoms.atom: How is heart rate calculated from an ECG?
atoms.definition: Method 1: Count QRS complexes in 10 seconds and multiply by 6. Method 2 (regular rhythm): Count large blocks between two R waves and divide into 300.

atoms.atom: What is Coronary Artery Disease (CAD)?
atoms.definition: Coronary Artery Disease, also known as ischemic heart disease, is a condition where plaque builds up in the coronary arteries, restricting blood flow to the heart. It is the leading cause of death globally, accounting for 16% of total deaths.

atoms.atom: What are the global mortality statistics for cardiovascular disease?
atoms.definition: According to WHO 2019 statistics, cardiovascular disease is the commonest cause of mortality accounting for 18.5 million deaths annually, far exceeding cancer (10 million) and respiratory diseases (under 4 million).

atoms.atom: What is the double burden of disease in South Africa and low/middle-income countries?
atoms.definition: The double burden refers to the simultaneous high prevalence of both infectious diseases (tuberculosis, AIDS) and non-communicable diseases (lifestyle-related conditions like CAD, hypertension, diabetes).

atoms.atom: What is the most lethal manifestation of Coronary Artery Disease?
atoms.definition: Myocardial infarction (heart attack) is the most lethal and dangerous manifestation of CAD. It is a medical emergency caused by complete or near-complete blockage of a coronary artery.

atoms.atom: What is the key message about CAD prevention and modification?
atoms.definition: CAD is a disease of lifestyle that can be modified through choices about diet and physical activity. It can also be managed with pharmacological therapies and mechanical interventions (angioplasty, stenting, bypass surgery). Leading a judicious lifestyle can delay or prevent CAD onset.

atoms.atom: What are the main clinical manifestations of CAD?
atoms.definition: The main manifestations are: (1) Stable/Chronic coronary syndrome (stable angina), (2) Acute coronary syndrome including ST-elevation MI and non-ST elevation MI, (3) Unstable angina, (4) Silent ischemia, and (5) Sudden cardiac death.

atoms.atom: What does ST segment depression of more than 1mm indicate, especially if horizontal?
atoms.definition: Highly suggestive of underlying coronary obstruction.

atoms.atom: What is the significance of ST elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI)?
atoms.definition: A medical emergency where myocytes are under threat and begin to die after 5 minutes without blood supply and oxygen delivery.

atoms.atom: What are the diagnostic criteria for ST elevation in limb leads vs chest leads for STEMI?
atoms.definition: Limb leads (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF): require at least 1mm elevation. Chest leads (precordial): require at least 2mm elevation.

atoms.atom: What does 'tombstone' pattern represent on ECG?
atoms.definition: A pattern of convex ST elevation (often 5-15mm) seen in anterior STEMI, named for its appearance.

atoms.atom: What is reciprocal ST depression in the context of myocardial infarction?
atoms.definition: ST depression in leads opposite to those showing ST elevation; occurs when infarction affects one region and helps confirm the diagnosis.

atoms.atom: When is exercise stress testing contraindicated?
atoms.definition: In very high-risk patients (with typical anginal symptoms and multiple risk factors) and very low-risk patients (young individuals with no risk factors), as it may provoke ischemic events or is unhelpful.

atoms.atom: What is the preferred fibrinolytic agent for STEMI, and why?
atoms.definition: Tenectoplase is preferred (most effective), followed by alteplase, then streptokinase. However, any fibrinolytic agent given timely is better than nothing. Tenectoplase is most expensive and not always available.

atoms.atom: What dual anti-platelet therapy is used in STEMI?
atoms.definition: Aspirin (chewed tablet for rapid absorption, then 100mg indefinitely) and clopidogrel loading dose 300mg followed by 75mg daily. Dual therapy continues for 1 year; then clopidogrel is stopped but aspirin continues for life.

atoms.atom: What anti-thrombin agent is preferred in STEMI and why?
atoms.definition: Enoxaparin is preferred over heparin because it is given subcutaneously (easier administration). Heparin requires continuous IV infusion and needs monitoring, making it more cumbersome.

atoms.atom: What is the minimum number of antithrombotic agents given in STEMI?
atoms.definition: A minimum of 4 antithrombotic agents: fibrinolytic agent + 2 antiplatelet agents (aspirin + clopidogrel) + 1 anticoagulant (enoxaparin or heparin).

atoms.atom: What is the success rate of primary angioplasty vs fibrinolytic therapy in STEMI?
atoms.definition: Primary angioplasty leads to 95% vessel opening in skilled hands. Fibrinolytic therapy achieves approximately 64% vessel patency.

atoms.atom: What is the timeline for endothelial incorporation of a metal stent?
atoms.definition: A metal stent is typically incorporated into the vessel wall by the endothelium over 1-3 months.

atoms.card_source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml
atoms.atom: What is rheumatic fever by definition?
atoms.definition: A systemic disease of childhood, often recurrent, that follows group A beta-hemolytic streptococcal infection. It is a diffuse inflammatory disease of connective tissue affecting the heart, blood vessels, joints, subcutaneous tissue, and central nervous system.

atoms.card_source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml
atoms.atom: What is the pathogenesis of rheumatic fever?
atoms.definition: Delayed immune response to infection with group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus. Typically has a latent period of 1-3 weeks, followed by antibody-induced immunological damage to heart valves, joints, subcutaneous tissue, and basal ganglia.

atoms.card_source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml
atoms.atom: What are the major criteria of the modified Jones criteria for rheumatic fever diagnosis?
atoms.definition:
1. Carditis (pancarditis affecting pericardium, myocardium, endocardium)
2. Polyarthritis (flitting arthritis affecting one joint then progressing to adjacent joints)
3. Subcutaneous nodules
4. Erythema marginatum
5. Sydenham's chorea

atoms.card_source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml
atoms.atom: What are the minor criteria of the modified Jones criteria for rheumatic fever?
atoms.definition:
1. Fever
2. Arthralgia
3. Elevated ESR and C-reactive protein
4. Leukocytosis
5. Prolonged PR interval on ECG (may indicate possible aortic regurgitation)

atoms.card_source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml
atoms.atom: What supporting evidence is used to confirm streptococcal infection in rheumatic fever?
atoms.definition:
1. Increased titers of antistreptococcal antibodies (ASOT and anti-DNase B)
2. Positive throat culture for group A streptococcal
3. Recent history of scarlet fever

atoms.card_source: Evaluation Of Valvular Heart Disease - CMED4IIM2 - 2026_pages-001-009.yml
atoms.atom: What is the treatment of choice for primary prevention of rheumatic fever?
atoms.definition: Benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units intramuscularly in a single dose, OR Penicillin V (pen VK) 500mg orally twice daily for 10 days. For penicillin-allergic patients: Erythromycin for 5-10 days.

guide.body:
# Cardiology — Complete Study Guide

## Contents

### Coronary Artery Disease (CAD)

worldwide. The most recent global mortality statistics by the World Health Organization, which was done in 2019, indicates that cardiovascular disease at the top bar graph in gray by far is the commonest cause of mortality accounting for 18 and a half million deaths annually. The second commonest cause of death is cancer at 10 million. And then we go to the respiratory diseases at just under four million. And you would notice that infectious diseases, which are the more prevalent causes in low-income countries, tuberculosis, just over a million deaths annually, and AIDS just under 900,000. The point to emphasize is that in South Africa, as in many low- and middle-income countries, has a double burden of disease. On the one hand, infectious diseases, and on the other, diseases of lifestyle or non-communicable diseases, which are becoming highly prevalent in middle and low-income countries. Just to put things in perspective, even during the COVID era, which accounted for a marked increase in global mortality, if you take statistics done at the end of 2021, ischemic heart disease even in the COVID era accounted for over 15 million deaths, followed by stroke, which constitutes part of cardiovascular diseases, accounting for just under 11% of deaths, and COVID-19 at just under 10%. So even in the COVID era, cardiovascular diseases remained the commonest cause of global mortality. A coronary artery disease is also known as ischemic heart disease, and amongst the cardiovascular diseases, coronary artery disease is the leading cause of death globally, individually responsible for 16% of total deaths. Not only is coronary artery disease the commonest cause of death, the most lethal manifestation of coronary artery disease is heart attacks, or what we term myocardial infarction, and which is a medical emergency. What's important to know is that coronary artery disease is a disease of lifestyle that can be modified, either by choices you make, the way you live in terms of diet and physical activity, but it can also be modified by pharmacological therapies, as well as mechanical therapies, what we call rivascularization, by means of angioplasty and stenting, or by past surgery. So there are multiple ways in which we manage the condition, but the point to emphasize is that it is a disease of lifestyle. If we lead a judicious life and would moderate contingencies in terms of dietary choices and physical activity, you can, to a large extent, delay the onset or prevent coronary artery disease. What are the clinical manifestations of coronary artery disease? The commonest is what we call stable angina, but it's now called a chronic coronary syndrome, and the reason why it's called chronic coronary syndrome, the disease starts in the early teen years, some believe even in neutral, depending on the mother's choice of diet, but certainly during times of war, when young individuals die, mostly those who are 19 and 20 years old, post-mortem performed on these individuals already show the early markings of disease in the form of atherosclerotic blocks. But the disease becomes manifest usually in the middle years, generally after the age of 35, and most commonly between the ages of 50, 60, 70, and 80 years. So we call it a previously stable angina, but now we refer to it as a chronic coronary syndrome. The second manifestation is acute coronary syndrome, and the most devastating manifestation that is treatable is a heart attack, what we call a ST elevation myocardial infarction. Also amongst the acute coronary syndromes are what we call non-ST elevation acute coronary syndrome or non-ST elevation myocardial infarction. It is an in fact, but it is not so time dependent in terms of prognosis as ST elevation. And the third category amongst the acute coronary syndromes is unstable angina. For practical purposes, any person who experiences ischemic type chest pain or angina for the very first time is labeled unstable until we know the cause that the patient manifests. Pardon the artery disease can manifest a silent ischemia, which is detected either on an exercise stress test and an ECG or at times imaging modalities such as CT scans of the coronary arteries, which can show plaque deposition or a coronary vessel occlusion. The last manifestation is sudden cardiac death. A proportion of patients will present with cardiac arrest and will succumb. And a category is what we call resuscitated cardiac arrest. So these are generally the manifestations of coronary artery disease. Importantly, they are risk factors. Because as I've mentioned, this is a disease of lifestyle and the risk factors are classified into non-modifiable and modifiable. But the modifiable risk factors is age. A coronary artery disease prevalence increases after the age of 35 years. And the lifetime risk of developing the disease in men after the age of 40 years is 49%. That means a man has a almost 50% chance of developing coronary artery disease after the age of 40 years and a female about 32% will go on to develop coronary artery disease in later years. So the older you are, the more likely you are to have the condition. Men are at an increased risk compared to women, but once women reach the menopause, the prevalence becomes equivalent between both genders. Ethnicity in our locality is certainly the Indian population is at increased risk. But other groups such as the Afrikaans population, who has familial hypodemia or certain sectors of the Jewish community of Ashkenazi descent or even people of Scottish descent. So there are certainly certain groups and certain ethnicities that have an increased risk. Family history is important. A family history of coronary artery disease below the age of 50 years indicates an increased risk of mortality from coronary artery disease. And this is a more pronounced in the male gender. If your father or brother was diagnosed with coronary artery disease before the age of 55 years, or your mother or sister diagnosed before 65 years of age, these are considered additional risk factors in that individual. Then they are modifiable risk factors, which are highly dependent, as I said earlier, on lifestyle choices. Hypertension being the most prevalent. Approximately one out of every three individuals globally have hypertension. Hypodemia is the second most common risk factor. And then diabetes mellitus. Type 2 diabetes as well as type 1, diabetes increases the rate of coronary artery disease by more than two and a half times compared to those without diabetes. So to emphasize, although hypertension and hypodemia may be more common, diabetes is a more severe condition, making an individual more prone to the disease, as well as having poorer outcomes once the individual has coronary artery disease. Obesity in itself is an independent risk factor. And obesity also increases the risk of developing the other risk factors that we've mentioned. The more likelihood you are having hypertension, hypodemia and type 2 diabetes. Smoking, that's well known. Non-smokers who are exposed to secondhand smoking have a 25 to 30% increased risk of coronary artery disease, compared to those who have never been exposed to cigarette smoking. Therefore, we ban smoking in public areas, not to protect the smoker, but to protect the non-smoker. Poor diet and importantly, it's added sugar and that comes in the form of common things such as soft drinks and sweetened beverages. And just consuming this on a regular basis is associated with more than a 20% higher risk of myocardial infarct. Certainly saturated and trans fats, as well as an excess of red meat or processed meats. Processed meats are sausage along all these fancy Hollywood chops and the likes because the fat content in these diets are really high. And importantly, as well is a sedentary lifestyle. So these are all the body fiber risk factors that as we said, by leading a judicious lifestyle, you can avoid having the source factors and decrease your overall incidence of coronary artery disease. Also, other less recognized risk factors. Firstly, is non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease or sciatic hepatitis is now considered the commonest cause of cirrhosis in the world. And it is directly related to obesity and especially abdominal obesity. Individuals who have chronic kidney disease certainly have a higher incidence of coronary artery disease. And once you reach the point of dialysis, the chances of dying of coronary artery disease exceed the risk of dying from chronic kidney disease per se. Inflammatory conditions also increase risk of coronary artery disease because coronary artery disease in itself is an underlying inflammatory process. Inflammatory conditions commonly associated as systemic lupus, rheumatoid, inflammatory bowel disease, and importantly also HIV infection. The infection in itself leading to increase thrombosis or vasculitis affecting the endothelia or HIV drug therapy which leads to abnormalities in metabolic conditions such as hypo-epidemia and diabetes. We have a risk cause that help us assess an individual's risk of developing coronary artery disease. We have this risk of charts because as I said, the disease is usually manifest in your later years but the risk starts early. So these risk scores, they are many but one of the oldest is the Framingham, takes into consideration several risk factors which is age, total cholesterol, HDL cholesterol, systolic blood pressure with a treated or untreated and also considering where the patient is a smoker. And then it gives you a 10-year likelihood of developing total cardiovascular disease which includes coronary artery disease, stroke, and peripheral artery disease. But these risk scores are not very useful in diabetics for those who, for example, have familial hypo-epidemia because just by the mere fact of having diabetes and familial hypo-epidemia, you immediately are at higher risk of developing coronary artery disease. So there's no point in scoring with the Framingham risk assessment chart. And just to give you an idea of what the chart looks like, you can get it online on the left hand side before the male gender, on the right female gender. And if your blood pressure, for example, is completely normal with systolic less than 120, you get minus 2 points, but the points increase incrementally with the greater the severity of blood pressure. For example, if a blood pressure is about 140, you get 2, but if it's about 160, you get 3 points. Similarly, if you're on treatment, if you're a smoker and looking at all these risk factors, as I said, it's clearly available, it helps the individual practitioner, especially at the primary care level in deciding if the individual is at higher risk or very high risk, then you can institute preventative measures. And if you look at the score, if your score is less than 3, you're low risk, it's less than 15, you're at moderate risk, but the moment your total points are more than 15 and especially more than 30, then we institute pharmacotherapy in addition to lifestyle measures to rigidly control the risk factors in order to minimize your risk of myocardial infarction and death. Individuals who are at very high risk of cardiovascular events do not require risk scoring, as I've mentioned. If an individual has established a prosclerotic disease, remember the heart and the coronary vascular literature is just one vascular bit. The exact same disease process can give you cerebrovascular disease or stroke and TIA or peripheral arterial disease, which is gangrene and predication, as well as aneurysms in the aorta. So if you have established a prosclerotic disease in one vascular bit, the likelihood of you having it in another vascular bit is high. So there's no point in individually risk scoring because your overall risk is high. If you have type 2 diabetes plus other risk factors, and this is common in the population, type 2 diabetes coupled with smoking or hypertension or dysepademia, or the mere fact that if you're diabetic and above the age of 40, you're already at high risk. If you have type 1 diabetes with any degree of protonuria, you're at high risk. If you have genetic or familial hyperlipidemia, which is common in our paphycana population of Dutch descent in South Africa, we have about 300,000 individuals. We have severe familial hyperlipidemia, and this means total cholesterol in excess of 7.5 millimolts per liter or an LDL cholesterol, LDL being the bad cholesterol in excess of 5 millimolts per liter, that automatically puts you at high risk. If you have severe chronic kidney disease, and by that we mean more than stage 4, stage 4 is GFR, less than 30 millimeters per liter, or stage 5, where your GFR is less than 15, what we term end-stage renal disease, you are also at high risk, very high risk of coronary artery disease. And lastly, asymptomatic individuals who may have CT scans for other reasons, whether it's lung disease or looking at abdominal CT scan, and they are atherosclerotic plaques demonstrated not only in the coronary vasculature, but other areas, and the common other areas are the aorta, and a knuckle of the aorta, or in the infra-renal abdominal aorta, which is the commonest site of atherosclerosis. And x-rays or CT scans can pick up a calcification or plaques at these sites. Now, just to emphasize the pathology, that's why we call it chronic coronary syndrome, because the disease is silent for many years, and as I indicated, evidence of fatty streak, which is lipid deposition within, this is a cross-section of a coronary artery, and this is a longitudinal section. The yellow bit is the plaque, which is lipid and inflammatory cells and smooth muscle cells that collect in the war of vessel and lead to progressive narrowing of vessel. And in the early years, and this is in your teen years, in your 20s, in your 30s, you already have disease, but this is clinically silent. But having said that, it's not uncommon in our environment to see young individuals, you know, because of the prevalence of obesity starting very early in the early teen years, and when you're obese, the accompanying elevation in blood pressure, or being diabetic or prediabetic, we are seeing individuals, and the youngest I've seen is a 19 year old with a heart attack. We've sent several individuals in their 20s for bypass, and we regularly perform stenting as well. So in the early years, the early teens, most people have the fatty streak. Then the plaque, as you enter your middle years, you get your late 30s, 40s, 50s, the plaque grows, and impinges more on the lumen of the vessel and narrows the vessel. It's only once the vessel is 70% narrowed. Do you get clinical manifestation of ischemic heart disease? And the commonest manifestation is ischemic chest pain, what we call angina. So that occurs pretty late in the course of this disease. And finally, acute coronary syndrome, which makes the patient most vulnerable to death, is when the plaque ruptures, and when it ruptures, thrombus is formed. And as you see, the yellow is the plaque with the lipid and inflammatory cell, and the red is the thrombus. The thrombus is the final harbinger of total vessel occlusion, and if the vessel is occluded, there's no downstream blood flow, and the myocytes downstream die, and that is myocardial infarct. So the point to emphasize is this is decades in the making. And in the early years, with the judicious lifestyle and lifestyle choices, you can delay the onset or prevent onset of coronary artery disease and infarction. Again, just to emphasize, in the early years, it's lipid and lymphocytes and smooth muscle cells, then this plaque ruptures, and it can form a clot. The clot can be small, in which case the body can take root, get rid of it, or it can be totally occlusive, blocking the entire lumen of the vessel. And if there's no blood supply and no oxygen downstream of the vessel, that part of the heart will die. If you're lucky, it will heal, and would therapy the vessel may be patent, it's not normal. Emphasizing again, treatment of this disease, it's lifelong. Now, chronic coronary syndrome, we have a plaque, and as I've mentioned, the plaque has a lipid and the inflammatory cells, and the plaque has to cause more than 50 or 70% lumen stenosis to limit blood flow downstream, and that will then manifest with ischemic chest pain or angina. In acute coronary syndromes, in stemi, the thrombus that forms on the plaque is large, it causes total occlusion of the vessel. That then becomes an emergency, because there's no blood flow downstream, and everything downstream will then begin to die, and the more proximal the occlusion, the bigger the impact, and the more ominous the prognosis. That is what we call acetylubation. In non-acetylubation acute coronary syndrome, the pathology is identical. This plaque that has been there for a long time may fissure rupture, a thrombus is formed, but here you're lucky in that the thrombus is not all occlusive. The 50 or 70% stenosis can become a 90% stenosis, but there is still some blood flow. So although you need to attend through this relatively urgently, it's not as an emergent condition as a stemi. The mechanism of infarction here is that bits of the clot break off and block off downstream smaller vessels. Now returning to chronic coronary syndromes, as I said, the first indicator of coronary artery disease is angina, and this occurs in 50% of patients. What do we mean by stable angina or chronic coronary syndrome? The definition of stable angina is that the symptom characteristics are unchanged for the preceding 60 days. I point this out to emphasize the difference between stable angina and unstable angina. Here, chronic coronary syndrome refers to stable angina, where the pattern of pain has not changed for the preceding 60 days. We diagnose angina by history and history alone. To emphasize angina is a diagnosis made on history, and in the next slide, I'll describe what we mean by angina. A 12-lead ECG may help. It may show a previous impact, or you can do a 12-lead ECG when you're exercising or any form of stress, and the greater the amount of exercise, the greater the ischemic requirements or the ischemic burden on the heart, and even though the ECG may start up normal with progressive exercise and more effort, you can get changes on the ECG. And the commonest stress test we do is the treadmill exercise stress test. We can use pharmacological stress with the rutamine, or we can do nuclear stress tests. We can also diagnose chronic coronary syndromes by CT, coronary angioplative. That looks at the plaque burden within a coronary artery, and it can also quantify the percentage stenosis, as well as comment on the amount of calcium within the vessel. Now, I said angina is a diagnosis made on history and history alone. What do we mean by typical angina? Typical angina. It's very likely to have obstructive coronary artery disease. The patient describes the chest pain, which is not necessarily pain. Therefore, we refer to it as discomfort, as a constriction in the front of the chest, and it may radiate to the neck, the jaw, the shoulder, the left arm, or both arms. So it's the type of pain, it's more constriction, and the pattern of radiation, that's one component. The second important component is that the chest pain is provoked by physical exertion, or some form of emotional stress. And it typically is relieved with rest, or with nitrates, which we spray into the mouth, or take a tablet sublingually. It's rapidly relieved with nitrates within five minutes. So typical angina has to satisfy all three criteria. The description of the pain, the pattern of radiation being provoked by physical exertion being relieved by rest or nitrates. Very quickly within five minutes, that is typical angina. A typical angina, which is probable angina, you meet two of the above three criteria. And non-anginal chest pain, when only one criterion of the formation three is satisfied. We also trade the severity of angina as we trade the severity of dyspnea. This is similar, but instead of being called the New York Art Association Classification of Dyspnea, this is the Canadian cardiovascular society, creating of angina, and it's very similar. A great one is angina only with a very strenuous exertion. Great two would moderate exertion, great three would mild exertion, and great voice angina at rest. So this is a universal terminology that we use to convey the severity of angina. And on the right hand side, you can see common descriptions of how we trade severity. Also, in addition to the history taking in terms of diagnosing chronic coronary syndrome or stable angina, there are clinical features that alert us to disease. Obviously, when we look at a patient, the age, the older age, male, gender, certain ethnic groups as we mentioned. If you're in a male in a middle age, then we know if you get chest pain, they always think of underlying ischemic or coronary artery disease. The presence of risk factors further strengthens your hand in making that diagnosis. So if the individual is 45, a male, smokes, is obese, and coupled with hypertension, diabetes is a hyperlipidemia. The more the risk factors, the more likely you are to have this disease. And obviously, if you have a presence of non-cordinately atherosclerotic disease, meaning you have evidence of disease or occlusive disease in other vascular bits, such as you may have had a previous stroke, or you have evidence of a carotid brewery, indicating a transient ischemic attack, or you may have low limb chlorication indicating peripheral vascular disease, or you may palpate an abdominal aortic aneurysm, or an ultrasound, or some imaging modality may incidentally pick up an aortic aneurysm. So all of these clinical factors should be looked for, because before you look at any investigate modality, it clearly will help you and guide you in the right direction that the cause of this chest pain is underlying ischemic heart disease. Now, the third way that we try to make the diagnosis and confirm the diagnosis is with a 12-lead ECG. So what we look at in the EC

---

### Coronary Artery Disease (CAD)

G is the ST segment. That is the P-wave, that is atrial depolarization, the QRS, which is ventricular depolarization, and after the S-wave, you get this ST segment and then the T-wave. It is the ST segment that really helps us when we try to make a diagnosis of coronary artery disease. This top ECG shows that this ST segment is isoelectric with the baseline. This is normal. If the baseline is here and the ST segment is depressed, and here we can see depressed by one, two, three little small squares, and it's horizontal ST depression. So any depression in excess of one millimeter, and especially if it's horizontal, that is highly suggestive of underlying coronary obstruction. But the most important and ominous is ST elevation, because ST elevation myocardial infarction is a medical emergency, where the myocyte is under threat, and after five minutes, if there is no blood supply and oxygen delivery, the myocyte and myocytes begin to die. So ST segment elevation and especially convex ST elevation. In the limb leads, limb leads are leads one, two, three, AVR, AVL, AVF. All you need is one millimeter. That means one little small square above the baseline or in the pre-chordial leads, meaning the chest leads, we want to be six. There's more than two millimeter ST segment concave elevation. That is highly suggestive of an ST elevation myocardial infarction which is, as I said, a medical emergency. So again, this is a R-rave, an ST elevation that is earlier on, and later on, the R-rave is completely lost and you get a deep QA and residual ST elevation. So when we see deep QAves, we know that that part of the heart has completely infected and the ST elevation is just a residual change that you see once the vessel is completely occluded. This is an ECG of somebody who is having an anterior ST elevation myocardial infarction. We see ST elevation in leads V1, V2, V3, V4. This pattern of ST elevation is called tombstock. It looks like a tombstock. It's marked. It's not two millimeters. This is like 5, 10, almost 15 millimeters of ST elevation. The anterior leads are V1 to V6 as well as one in AVL. So depending way in the coronary artery, the vessel is blocked. It may be in the entire anterior segment, which is one AVL V1 to V6, or it could be just in the septal leads or V6, V5, V6, the apical leads. When you get ST segment elevation infarction, you often get reciprocal ST depression in the opposite leads. So if you're having an anterior infar, what may help you confirm that there is a problem you get reciprocal ST depression in the inferior leads. Now in this case, you get ST segment elevation in the inferior leads, which leads to 3 and AVL, and you get reciprocal ST depression in the anterior leads, which is one AVL. You can see it ST depression V1 and to a less extent in V2. But the important thing to note is the ST elevation in the inferior leads. So this is an inferior impact, and that is how the ECG can be very helpful. Okay. To diagnose an infarct, where it's a permanent ST elevation, to help you diagnose a non-ST elevation where it could be transient, or in a patient with so-called stable angina, who is at higher risk. If you do, for example, an exercise stress test, which may be demonstrating a normal ECG when you start when the patient is at risk, is at risk, but with progressive exercise, as heart rate and blood pressure and effort increases, you may get transient ST elevation, and that's an indication to immediately stop because you can provoke infarction. So we use the exercise stress test to help us establish a diagnosis if there's any doubt, it also helps us determine a risk of a coronary event. By that, we mean there are seven stages to an exercise stress test. The standard protocol we use is a Brusport protocol, where every three minutes the treadmill goes faster and the incline increases. So everybody should be able to at least complete stages one and two. So stage one is three minutes, it's walking on the flat at the low pace. Stage two is still very slight incline and walking at the slightly faster pace. Most individuals should be able to complete stage one stage two, which is six minutes of exercise. If you're unable to do six minutes, then you're very compromised and the test may not be helpful, but then it also gives you an indication that this patient is very deconditioned or has a significant underlying clinical disease. It's important to mention that the exercise stress test is not of value in patients at very high risk or very low risk. By very high risk, if you've got an angina-addressed typical symptoms and you have multiple risk factors, by performing an exercise stress test, you can provoke an ischemic event such as an acute coronary syndrome. Or if you have chest pain in a very young individual, you're 20, a female, you've got absolutely no risk factor. The chest pain then is likely due to something else. You may have a viral infection, whether it's a flu and if it's a bad flu that goes on to a pericarditis that may mimic ischemic chest pain or may at times even have concave ST elevation. The exercise stress test is not of much value or not very safe and very high risk, and as I say, if you're young and don't have any risk factors, it's not much point in performing an exercise stress test. Will you interpret an exercise stress test? You should look at symptoms because most patients, we said angina or ischemic chest pain is provoked by exercise. So, if you get chest pain, typical chest pain, while you're exercising, that will help you with the diagnosis. If you're unable to exercise for at least six minutes, we know you likely have a problem unless you're very unfit. We look at hemodynamic response, by that we mean heart rate and blood pressure. The more you exercise, the progressive increase you have in blood pressure. If your blood pressure begins to fall, you know, that's an ominous indication, or you're unable to increase your heart rate, then that is also problematic. In the presence of persistent or uncontrolled infection, wild receiving antibiotics would be an indication for surgery. Local superiority of complications, perevalvular myocardial abscesses as well as patients with narcotic aneurysms would require surgery. The indications for surgery in patients with native valve, endocaditis and prosthetic valve endocaditis are essentially the same. Surgery is warranted for patients with active endocaditis who have one or more of the following complications as we mentioned earlier, the heart failure, persistent infection, and recurrent emboline. Relative indications for surgery would include evidence of perevalvular infection such as intracardiac abscess or fistula formation, a rupture of a sinus of balsalva aneurysm, fungal endocaditis, endocaditis which is secondary to a highly resistant microorganism and a relapse after a course of adequate antibiotic therapy particularly in those patients with prosthetic valves. Another indication would be culture negative endocaditis with fever more than 10 days after starting empiric therapy. Some of the echo features for surgical indication. If one notes persistent vegetation after a major systemic embolic episode, this would be an indication for surgery. The size of the vegetation, if it's more than one centimeter in diameter, then this would warrant surgical treatment. And if there's an increase in vegetation size, despite four weeks of antibiotics, as well as acute mitral insufficiency, if there's a valve perforation or rupture and any peri-annular extension of the infection. The outcome of surgery fortunately in patients with infected endocaditis has been good, particularly when the surgical treatment is radical with removal of all the infected and necrotic tissue. In a recent study of 138 patients who underwent bowel surgery in the presence of active infection, the early mortality due to heart failure or septic multi-organ failure was 11.5%. Risk factors for early mortality were NYHA-plus-4, pathogenic shock, advanced age, or pre-operative acute renal failure, and staphylococcal infection. Now, to discuss some of the neurological complications, patients with heart failure, uncontrolled infection, they have an absence of a high embolic risk. The scan result in embolic phenomena with resulting intracranial hemorrhage, coma, as well as stroke. And if this is the case as a complication, then these patients should be managed conservatively and treated. In the absence of those complications in surgery, it must be considered. Now, to just discuss the prophylaxis, the rationale, endocaditis usually follows bacteremia. Risk of endocaditis is small. There's no proof of efficacy or preventative therapy, but recommended for patients with predisposing cardiac lesions or undergoing procedures known to cause bacteremia. Those patients with valvular heart disease, congenital heart disease, and intra cardiac prosthesis, as well as somebody who previously had endocaditis. The principle of antibiotic prophylaxis when performing procedures to patients who are at risk, of endocaditis with predisposing cardiac lesions are maintained. The prophylaxis of antibiotics is limited to patients at the highest risk, undergoing high risk dental procedures. Of course, good dental hygiene and regular dental review are more important than prophylaxis in preventing endocaditis. Of course, aseptic measures during venous catheterization and any invasive procedure is mandatory. This is again from the European Society of Cardiology procedures at highest risk of infected endocaditis. Antibotic prophylaxis is not recommended for respiratory tract procedures, GI procedures, and skin and soft tissue procedures. The recommended prophylaxis for dental procedures for those at risk, if there's no allergy to penicillin or empicillin, then either amoxicillin or empicillin, two grams oral OIVs recommended in adults, and in children 50 milligrams per kg, at the oral or IV. In the presence of penicillin allergy, then clinder mycin, for adults 2 grams oral or intravenous, and for children 20 milligrams per kg, oral or intravenous. Thank you.

100,000 heart valves are implanted annually in the United States IE develops in 1 to 4 % of valve recipients during the 1st year following valve replacement, and in approximately 1 percent per year thereafter RISK FACTORS Nosocomial endocarditis Usually a complication of bacteremia induced by an invasive procedure or a vascular device Structural heart disease Approximately three-fourths of all patients with IE have a preexisting structural cardiac abnormality Congenital heart disease is present in 10-20% cases The most common predisposing congenital heart lesions are bicuspid aortic valves, PDA, VSD, coarctation of the aorta, and tetralogy of Fallot RISK FACTORS Degenerative valvular lesions The risk of IE in patients with MVP and associated regurgitation is estimated to be 5 to 8 times higher than that in the normal population Aortic valve disease(stenosis or/and regurgitation) is present in 12 to 30 percent of cases RISK FACTORS History of infective endocarditis Recurrent endocarditis occurred in 4.5 percent of one large cohort of non-addicts Other studies have reported rates of IE recurrence ranging from 2.5 to 9 percent HIV infection A number of cases of IE have been reported in patients with HIV infection It has been suggested that HIV infection is an independent risk factor for IE in IV drug abusers A number of other, less common predisposing factors for IE include Pregnancy AV fistulas used for hemodialysis Central venous and pulmonary artery catheters Peritoneovenous shunts for the control of ascites Ventriculoatrial shunts for the management of hydrocephalus In addition, patients with ulcerative lesions of the colon due to carcinoma or inflammatory bowel disease have a poorly understood predilection to develop endocarditis secondary to Strep.bovis PATHOPHYSIOLOGY Localization of IE is related to: high pressure areas down stream from sites where blood flows at high velocity through a narrow orifice Transient bacteremia Occurs whenever a mucosal surface heavily colonized with bacteria is traumatized If preexistent NBTE, it may result in colonization and IE DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH TO IE History A careful history should be performed with special attention given to a history of prior cardiac lesions and historical clues pointing toward a recent source of bacteremia Physical examination A meticulous clinical examination should be performed looking for clinical evidence of small and large emboli with special attention to the fundi, conjunctivae, skin, and digits Cardiac examination may reveal signs of new regurgitant murmurs and signs of CHF Neurologic evaluation may detect evidence of focal neurologic impairment CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS Contributed by these processes: The infectious process on the valve, including the local intracardiac complications Bland or septic embolization to any organ Constant bacteremia Circulating immune complexes CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS Fever, rarely >400c (>95%) Nonspecific symptoms (weakness, weight loss, night sweats) Audible heart murmur (>85% of cases) Petechiae (20-40%) Osler nodes (10-25%) Janeway lesions (<5%) Splinter hemorrhages (10-30%) Roth spots (<5%) Clubbing (10-20%) Splenomegaly (25-60%) Musculoskeletal manifestations (25-45%) Major embolic episodes (>30%) Neurologic deficits LAB FINDINGS Hematology Anemia: normochromic, normocytic, low serum iron, low iron-binding capacity (70-90%) Thrombocytopenia (5-15%) Leukocytosis (20-30%) Histiocytes (>25%) Elevated ESR, with mean value of 57mm/hr (90-100%) Hypergammaglobulinemia (20-30%) Urinalysis Proteinuria (50-65%) Microscopic hematuria (30-60%) Red cell casts (12%)

LAB FINDINGS Serology Rheumatoid factor (40-50%) Circulating immune complexes Antinuclear antibodies Elevated levels of serum globulins Presence of cryoglobulins Hypocomplementemia False positive serologic tests for syphilis Blood culture Most important lab test Positive cultures in 97% of cases A minimum of three blood cultures should be obtained over a time period based upon the severity of the illness Electrocardiogram All patients with suspected IE should have an ECG to determine whether there is evidence of heart block or a conduction delay and to establish a baseline should such a complication develop later PROCEDURES Echo TTE is rapid, noninvasive specificity: 98% sensitivity: <60% TEE higher ultrasonic frequencies, improve spatial resolution specificity: 94% (prosthetic valve: 88-100%) sensitivity: 76-100% (prosthetic valve: 86-94%) Echocardiography Should be performed in all patients with suspected IE A TTE should initially be obtained in patients with native heart valves, while those with prosthetic valves should undergo TEE Detection of a vegetation by TTE is a positive test However, a negative study does not preclude the diagnosis and should be followed by TEE, when there is an intermediate or high suspicion of IE Duke Criteria for IE diagnosis Major criteria Positive blood culture for infective endocarditis Typical microorganism for IE from 2 separte blood cultures Viridans streptococci, Streptococcus bovis, HACEK group or, Community-acquired staphylococcus aureus or enterococci, in the absence of a primary focus, Single positive culture for C burnetti or ab. titre against phase 1 >1:800 or Persistently pos. blood cultures - 2 culture sets drawn >12 hr apart All of 3, or majority of 4 or more separate blood cultures, with first and last specimens drawn at least 1 hour apart Evidence of endocardial involvement Findings on echo positive for IE Oscillating intracardiac mass on valve or supporting structures or in the path of regurgitant jets, or on iatrogenic devices, in the absence of an alternative anatomic explanation, or Abscess, or New partial dehiscence of prosthetic valve, or New valvular regurgitation Duke Criteria for IE diagnosis Minor criteria Predisposition: predisposing heart condition or intravenous drug use Fever: >38°c Vascular phenomena: arterial embolism, septic pulmonary infarcts, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial hemorrhage, Janeway lesions Immunological phenomena: glomerulnephritis, Osler nodes, Roth spots, rheumatoid factor Echocardiogram: findings consistent with IE but not meeting major criterion above Microbiologic evidence: positive blood culture but not meeting major criterion above, or serologic evidence of active infection with organism consistent with IE DIAGNOSIS OF IE Definite 2 Major criteria or 1 major and 3 minor criteria or 5 minor criteria Possible 1 major and 1 minor criteria or 3 minor criteria

INDICATIONS SURGICAL THERAPY Refractory CHF directly related to valve dysfunction Physiologically significant valve dysfunction as demonstrated by echo >2 serious systemic embolic episode particularly in the presence of large vegetations Persistent or uncontrolled infection while receiving appropriate antimicrobial therapy Ineffective antimicrobial therapy Local suppurative complications including perivalvular or myocardial abscesses Resection of mycotic aneurysms INDICATIONS FOR SURGERY IN IE The indications for surgery in patients with native-valve IE and prosthetic-valve IE are essentially the same Surgery is warranted for patients with active IE who have one or more of the following complications: CHF that is directly related to valve dysfunction Persistent or uncontrolled infection while receiving appropriate antimicrobial therapy, including evidence of perivalvular extension Recurrent emboli, >2, particularly in the presence of large vegetations INDICATIONS FOR SURGERY IN IE Relative indications for surgery Evidence of perivalvular infection, such as intracardiac abscess or fistula formation Rupture of a sinus of Valsalva aneurysm Fungal endocarditis Endocarditis due to highly resistant microorganism Relapse after a course of adequate antimicrobial therapy, particularly in prosthetic valve endocarditis Culture-negative IE with fever more than 10 days after starting empirical therapy INDICATIONS FOR SURGERY IN PROSTHETIC VALVE IE Same as native valve endocarditis Perivalvular infection Valve Dehiscence excessively mobile prosthesis on echo results in hemodynamic instability SURGICAL THERAPY: ECHO FEATURES Persistent vegetations after a major systemic embolic episode Large (>1cm diameter) anterior mitral valve vegetation Increase in vegetation size 4 weeks after antibiotic therapy Acute mitral insufficiency Valve perforation or rupture Periannular extension of infection OUTCOME OF SURGERY The outcome of surgery in patients with IE has been good, particularly when surgical treatment is radical with the removal of all infected and necrotic tissue In a recent study of 138 patients who underwent valve surgery in the presence of active infection, the early mortality, due to heart failure or septic multiorgan failure, was 11.5 % Risk factors for early mortality were NYHA class IV or cardiogenic shock, advanced age, preoperative acute renal failure, and staphylococcal infection Operation for infective endocarditis: Results after implantation of mechanical valves. Ann Thorac Surg 1998; 65:359.

Evaluation of Valvular Heart Disease Dr. D.R. Naidoo Department of Cardiology Rheumatic Fever DEFINITION: Acute rheumatic fever is a systemic disease of childhood ,often recurrent that follows group A beta hemolytic streptococcal infection It is a diffuse inflammatory disease of connective tissue primarily involving heart, blood vessels, joints, subcut.tissue and CNS Pathogenesis Delayed immune response to infection with group A beta hemolytic streptococci. After a latent period of 1-3 weeks, antibody induced immunological damage occur to heart valves, joints, subcutaneous tissue & basal ganglia of brain. Step I - Primary Prevention (eradication of streptococci) Step II - anti inflammatory treatment (aspirin,steroids) Step III- supportive management & management of complications Step IV- Secondary Prevention (prevention of recurrent attacks) Treatment STEP I: Primary Prevention of Rheumatic Fever (Treatment of Streptococcal Tonsillopharyngitis) Agent Dose Mode Duration Benzathine penicillin G 0.6M IU ( 27Kg) Intramuscular Once 1.2 M IU (>27 kg ) or Penicillin V Children: 250 mg bd/tds Oral 10 d (phenoxymethyl penicillin) Adolescents and adults: 500 mg 2-3 times daily For individuals allergic to penicillin Erythromycin: 20-40 mg/kg/d bd/qid (max 1g/d) Oral 10 d Azithromycin 500mg (day1) 250mg/d for 4/7 Oral 5 d or Ethylsuccinate 40 mg/kg/d bd/qid Oral 10 d (maximum 1 g/d) Step II: Anti inflammatory treatment Arthritis Aspirin 75-100mg/kg/d qid for 6/52 -Rapid resolution of fever & arthralgia -But incidence of RHD not decreased Carditis Prednisone 2 - 2.5mg/kg/d bd for 2/52 (Taper over 2/52 while introducing Aspirin) -Reduce inflammatory respone(fever & acute phase reactants) -Little objective evidence for rapid resolution of cardiac compromise -Should be reserved for acute severe carditis 3.Step III: Supportive management & management of complications Bed rest Treatment of congestive cardiac failure: -digitalis,diuretics Treatment of chorea: -diazepam or haloperidol Rest to joints & supportive splinting STEP IV : Secondary Prevention of Rheumatic Fever (Prevention of Recurrent Attacks) Agent Dose Mode Benzathine penicillin G 1 200 000 U every 4 weeks* Intramuscular or Penicillin V 250 mg twice daily Oral or Sulfadiazine 0.5 g once daily for patients 27 kg Oral 1.0 g once daily for patients >27 kg For individuals allergic to penicillin and sulfadiazine Erythromycin 250 mg twice daily Oral *In high-risk situations, administration every 3 weeks is justified and recommended Recommendations of American Heart Association

Anatomy The pericardium is the membranous sac surrounding the heart Consists of two layers: inner visceral layer, and outer parietal layer, which is continuous with the diaphragm and outer walls of great arteries Pericardial cavity consists of 50mls of fluid Normal functions Maintaining an optimal cardiac shape Reducing friction between the beating heart and adjacent structures Protecting the heart from other diseases which are present in adjacent organs Preventing overfilling of the heart Acute pericarditis Is an inflammation of the pericardium Variety of causes Causes symptoms, signs and investigations History Clinical examination CXR ECG Echo Invasive investigations

Evaluation of Valvular Heart Disease Dr. D.R. Naidoo Department of Cardiology Rheumatic Fever DEFINITION: Acute rheumatic fever is a systemic disease of childhood ,often recurrent that follows group A beta hemolytic streptococcal infection It is a diffuse inflammatory disease of connective tissue primarily involving heart, blood vessels, joints, subcut.tissue and CNS Pathogenesis Delayed immune response to infection with group A beta hemolytic streptococci. After a latent period of 1-3 weeks, antibody induced immunological damage occur to heart valves, joints, subcutaneous tissue & basal ganglia of brain. Step I - Primary Prevention (eradication of streptococci) Step II - anti inflammatory treatment (aspirin,steroids) Step III- supportive management & management of complications Step IV- Secondary Prevention (prevention of recurrent attacks) Treatment STEP I: Primary Prevention of Rheumatic Fever (Treatment of Streptococcal Tonsillopharyngitis) Agent Dose Mode Duration Benzathine penicillin G 0.6M IU ( 27Kg) Intramuscular Once 1.2 M IU (>27 kg ) or Penicillin V Children: 250 mg bd/tds Oral 10 d (phenoxymethyl penicillin) Adolescents and adults: 500 mg 2-3 times daily For individuals allergic to penicillin Erythromycin: 20-40 mg/kg/d bd/qid (max 1g/d) Oral 10 d Azithromycin 500mg (day1) 250mg/d for 4/7 Oral 5 d or Ethylsuccinate 40 mg/kg/d bd/qid Oral 10 d (maximum 1 g/d) Step II: Anti inflammatory treatment Arthritis Aspirin 75-100mg/kg/d qid for 6/52 -Rapid resolution of fever & arthralgia -But incidence of RHD not decreased Carditis Prednisone 2 - 2.5mg/kg/d bd for 2/52 (Taper over 2/52 while introducing Aspirin) -Reduce inflammatory respone(fever & acute phase reactants) -Little objective evidence for rapid resolution of cardiac compromise -Should be reserved for acute severe carditis 3.Step III: Supportive management & management of complications Bed rest Treatment of congestive cardiac failure: -digitalis,diuretics Treatment of chorea: -diazepam or haloperidol Rest to joints & supportive splinting STEP IV : Secondary Prevention of Rheumatic Fever (Prevention of Recurrent Attacks) Agent Dose Mode Benzathine penicillin G 1 200 000 U every 4 weeks* Intramuscular or Penicillin V 250 mg twice daily Oral or Sulfadiazine 0.5 g once daily for patients 27 kg Oral 1.0 g once daily for patients >27 kg For individuals allergic to penicillin and sulfadiazine Erythromycin 250 mg twice daily Oral *In high-risk situations, administration every 3 weeks is justified and recommended Recommendations of American Heart Association
